title: What do circular cities do? Insights from 26 case studies
methods: Medium-N qualitative multiple-case study combining document analysis of 26 publicly available circular city strategies (filtered from an initial pool of 255 cities across four sources) with 18 semi-structured expert interviews with CE practitioners involved in strategy development or implementation; qualitative content analysis (Mayring, 2015) in Atlas.ti with ChatGPT-assisted clustering of thematically related quotes, followed by inductive cross-case comparison along two dimensions (scope of target sectors, scale of intervention) to deduce a 2x2 matrix of four archetypes validated against the interviews
industry: Municipal/public-sector circular economy strategies across sectors including built environment, food, procurement, waste, mobility, water, and energy
country: 26 cities predominantly in Europe and North America (e.g., Amsterdam, Freiburg, Leuven, Paris, Maribor, Prague, Luxembourg, Espoo, Helsinki, Charlotte, Cleveland, Lille, Murcia, Richmond, Turku, West Midlands)
time_period: Most recent publicly available city circular strategies as of the study; literature review of 74 articles; 18 expert interviews conducted by the research team
units: City circular economy strategies (cities/municipal units) plus expert interviewees
authors: Schuppler, M.; Bidmon, C.; Roelfsema, M.; van Vuuren, D.; Kirchherr, J.
theories: circular economy; sustainability transitions; typology/archetype theorizing
keywords: Circular city; Circular city archetypes; Circular economy; Industrial ecology; Sustainability; Urban circularity
research_question: How can cities be systematically and conceptually grouped based on the empirical reality of their CE measures? The study aims to advance theoretical understanding of circular cities by proposing an empirically grounded typology and to offer practical guidance for cities developing or strengthening their circular initiatives.
mechanism_summary: The authors argue that because urban circularity depends on locally available resources, institutional capacities, and sectoral priorities, cities translate generic CE principles into divergent strategic configurations. By coding 26 strategies against sectors targeted, 10R principles, enablers, and actions and cross-validating them with 18 expert interviews, the authors inductively derive two orthogonal dimensions — sectoral scope and scale of intervention — that partition cities into four archetypes. The archetypes differ in which sectors they prioritize, whether they emphasize higher- or lower-R principles, and whether they proceed through incremental cooperation or transformative innovation, producing distinct pathways for translating CE principles into urban practice.
theoretical_contribution: The study expands the theoretical conceptualization of the circular city from a single, ambiguous construct to at least four empirically grounded archetypes, demonstrating that urban circularity is not monolithic but a continuum of locally tailored strategies. It clarifies key CC dimensions and boundaries — notably sectoral scope and scale of intervention — supporting more rigorous theoretical development in a field previously marked by conceptual ambiguity, limited empirical depth, and the absence of a structured typology.
practical_implication: The four archetypes offer city managers a lens for understanding where their city stands within the broader landscape of circular transition, enabling them to benchmark their approach against similar cities and to identify transferable practices from detailed examples in the supplementary appendices. The authors also provide a self-assessment tool (Appendix A of SI 1) that allows practitioners to determine which archetype best fits their city, making strategic pathways more tangible and accessible for cities beginning to explore circularity.
limitations: The analysis predominantly covers European and North American cities, limiting global representativeness of the typology. The data reflect strategic intentions expressed in publicly available documents rather than on-the-ground outcomes, so whether a city ultimately reduces resource consumption or emissions may depend on political leadership changes or external funding not captured in the strategies. An initial classification into one archetype should not be seen as prescriptive or permanent, since cities operate in dynamic contexts and may transition between archetypes.
future_research: Future research should use the four archetypes as a starting point to investigate archetype-specific barriers, opportunities, and development trajectories, ultimately testing whether these strategic classifications translate into tangible resource and emission reductions. Additional work is needed to extend the typology beyond European and North American contexts and to examine transitions between archetypes over time as cities' conditions, priorities, and capacities evolve.
abstract: Cities produce the largest share of global carbon emissions yet are well-positioned to lead the circular economy (CE) transition. Research on circular cities (CC), a relatively new field, is characterized by conceptual ambiguity, limited empirical depth, and a lack of nuanced understanding of the concept. This medium-N study expands the concept of CCs, demonstrating that urban circularity is not monolithic but a continuum of locally tailored strategies. It analyzes the circular strategies of 26 cities, complemented by 18 expert interviews. Based on target sector scope and intervention scale, it identifies four CC archetypes: niche community optimizers (NCO), implementing promotional and educational measures in a few highimpact sectors, mainly built environment and procurement; diversified transformative enablers (DTE), pursuing innovative transformative measures across a high number of sectors, notably food, built environment, and procurement; diversified collaborative loopers (DCL), incrementally upgrading multiple “traditional” sectors through cooperation with public and private actors, notably built environment, waste, food, and procurement; and niche conformers (NC), refining existing practices mainly in the built environment and food sectors. This proposed typology advances the understanding of the CC concept in the literature to at least four archetypes and provides city managers with a tool to understand their city's position and to compare approaches with similar cities, promoting focused learning and knowledge exchange.
apa_citation: Schuppler, M., Bidmon, C., Roelfsema, M., van Vuuren, D., & Kirchherr, J. (2026). What do circular cities do? Insights from 26 case studies. *Journal of Industrial Ecology*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s44498-026-00018-y

title: Corporate Strategy and the Portfolio-Based View of CSR: Toward a Theory of Optimal CSR Diversification
methods: Conceptual theory development; integrates corporate diversification theory with the portfolio-based view of CSR to derive propositions and a decision-tree framework; no empirical data
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
authors: Skandera, D. J.; DesJardine, M. R.; Blevins, D. P.
theories: Penrosean resource-based theory; corporate diversification theory; portfolio-based view of CSR; stakeholder-based rewards / demand-side CSR
keywords: CSR portfolio; corporate social responsibility; corporate strategy; demand complementarities; demand-side; diversification; portfolio-based view of CSR; resource fungibility; supply-side
research_question: Given additional resources for CSR, how should managers allocate these resources within their firm's CSR portfolio to maximize the portfolio's financial performance?
mechanism_summary:
Not reported in paper

Drawing on corporate diversification theory and Penrosean resource-based logic, the authors argue that CSR's financial returns depend jointly on how stakeholders reward a firm's CSR efforts (demand side) and on how efficiently the firm can leverage and redeploy resources across initiatives (supply side). As firms add CSR investments within an existing initiative, within-cause domain, or unrelated cause, stakeholder rewards diminish at the margin while diversification costs rise due to coordination, integration, and resource-reallocation frictions. The relative steepness of these reward and cost curves is shaped by demand complementarities across initiatives and by the fungibility of the firm's underlying resources, which together determine which of the three investment options yields the highest marginal financial return at each stage of a CSR portfolio's evolution. Optimal CSR diversification therefore occurs at inflection points where marginal rewards equal marginal costs, yielding a set of decision rules for orchestrating CSR portfolios.
theoretical_contribution: The paper reframes CSR portfolio management as a diversification problem, complementing and challenging demand-centric views of CSR by integrating supply-side resource constraints and portfolio-level inefficiencies with stakeholder-based rewards. It distinguishes three CSR investment options (CSR portfolio scaling, within-cause diversification, and cross-cause diversification) and parses CSR portfolio depth into scaling versus within-cause diversification, adding granularity to prior breadth/depth conceptualizations. In doing so, it develops propositions and decision rules showing how demand complementarities and resource fungibility jointly determine when each option maximizes financial returns, opening a new "portfolio-based view of CSR" research agenda.
practical_implication: Managers should approach CSR investment decisions with the same diversification logic they apply to selecting product markets—evaluating each new initiative against both expected stakeholder rewards and the costs it creates within the existing portfolio. The framework advises knowing when to scale existing initiatives, diversify within a social issue area, or diversify into a new cause; strategically sharing and redeploying resources across initiatives; and considering cross-initiative complementarities in both resource requirements and stakeholder demand. External stakeholders (investors, customers, CSR evaluators) should likewise look beyond aggregate CSR scores to assess the synergies, coherence, and resource alignments across a firm's CSR portfolio.
limitations: As a conceptual paper, the framework is not empirically tested and its scope is bounded to CSR portfolio assembly, leaving CSR contractions/divestitures and dynamic portfolio management outside the model. The theorization focuses on resource fungibility as the key supply-side factor without incorporating other resource characteristics such as decomposability and scalability, and treats social issue characteristics (scale, salience, urgency, polarization) only in stylized form.
future_research: Future work should empirically test the propositions and extend the framework to CSR portfolio divestitures and dynamic, intertemporal CSR portfolio management, as well as incorporate additional resource characteristics such as decomposability and non-scale-free resources that may create sharper diversification trade-offs. Researchers could also examine how social issue attributes (scale, salience, urgency, stakeholder polarization) shape the tipping points between scaling, within-cause, and cross-cause diversification, and explore the societal-level implications of CSR "division of labor" across firms with distinct resource profiles.
abstract: Prior research suggests firms maximize the financial returns from corporate social responsibility (CSR) by focusing on how stakeholders reward their CSR investments. This demand-side view of CSR’s financial returns overlooks the costs tied to each CSR investment, neglecting that returns also depend on the efficient use of resources. We integrate insights from corporate strategy research on firm diversification with the portfolio-based view of CSR to take a step toward a theory of optimal CSR diversification that balances demand-side and supply-side considerations. Our theory treats CSR investments as a diversification problem in which managers must choose from among three investment options when allocating additional resources to CSR: CSR portfolio scaling, within-cause diversification, and cross-cause diversification. Which option yields the highest marginal financial return at any point in a CSR portfolio’s evolution depends on the diminishing marginal rewards from stakeholders and the increasing marginal diversification costs associated with each option. Crucially, demand-side (e.g., demand complementarities) and supply-side (e.g., resource fungibility) factors shape the rates at which marginal rewards and costs change over successive CSR investments. Our framework reveals new ways for firms to diversify CSR portfolios and enhance the financial returns from CSR investments by optimizing resource allocations across the initiatives comprising CSR portfolios.
apa_citation: Skandera, D. J., DesJardine, M. R., & Blevins, D. P. (2026). Corporate strategy and the portfolio-based view of CSR: Toward a theory of optimal CSR diversification. *Academy of Management Review*. https://doi.org/10.5465/amr.2023.0416

title: A Design for All: De-Neurotypicalizing Business Schools and Achieving Substantive Performativity
methods: Conceptual essay; theoretical argumentation drawing on the performativity, neurodiversity, and business school literatures
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
authors: Spoor, J. R.; Jameson, T. P.; Billsberry, J.; Vogus, T. J.
theories: performativity theory
keywords: business schools; de-neurotypicalization; diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI); neurodivergent; neurodiversity; neurotypical; performativity; substantive performativity; symbolic performativity; universal design
research_question: The essay addresses the open conceptual challenge of how organizations can realize the radical implications of performativity and transition from symbolic to substantive performativity, focused specifically on how business schools can move beyond symbolic DEI gestures to substantively redress the exclusion of neurodivergent students, staff, and faculty.
mechanism_summary: The paper articulates a conceptual mechanism rather than an empirical one. The authors argue that applying universal design principles — Universal Design for Learning (UDL) in the classroom and Universal Design for Work (UDW) in the workplace — drives a process they call de-neurotypicalization, which challenges and changes the implicit neurotypical assumptions embedded in business school culture, curriculum, teaching, and work practices. De-neurotypicalization, in turn, enables business schools to move from symbolic performativity (declarations, awareness campaigns, narrow accommodations) to substantive performativity that alters core structures and processes. The mechanism is theorized as one in which deep analysis of an underrecognized problem (neurotypical business schools) surfaces anomalies, generates paradigm shifts (e.g., regarding effective leadership), and enables practice shifts (e.g., new pedagogical practice and broader conceptions of accommodation through universal design).
theoretical_contribution: The authors develop the novel concept of de-neurotypicalization to illustrate how specific mechanisms (e.g., universal design) enable organizations to intentionally design for substantive performativity, building upon Marti and Gond (2018) to show how a new approach challenges existing practices, surfaces anomalies, and generates paradigm and practice shifts. They advance theory on DEI in business schools by illustrating the importance of intentional design and creating capabilities (e.g., universal design) that enable DEI efforts to be bundled rather than fragmented. The essay also provides the foundations for a fuller theory of the double empathy problem by showing how design choices, content choices, and norms can exacerbate it and how ad hoc, individualized interventions fail to redress it.
practical_implication: Business schools should apply universal design principles to both learning (UDL) and work (UDW) — including transparent workload allocation, flexible work arrangements, explicit feedback structures, multiple means of participation, and varied forms of assessment — to move from symbolic to substantive performativity on neuroinclusion. By embedding these practices and aligning internal workplace practices with classroom pedagogy, business schools can simultaneously improve outcomes for neurodivergent students, staff, and faculty, benefit neurotypical members of the community, and better prepare future leaders to build neuroinclusive organizations. Doing so also positions business schools to meet AACSB and stakeholder expectations for meaningful DEI commitment.
limitations: Not reported in paper.
future_research: Future empirical research should examine how symbolic performativity (e.g., declaring support for neurodiversity) and substantive performativity (e.g., neuroinclusive curriculum, pedagogy, and workplace practices) affect disclosure, neurodivergent enrolment and employment, and the growth of neurodivergent employment over time. Further conceptual and empirical work is needed to test the proposed mechanism of universal design. Researchers should also examine the extent to which the proposed benefits of de-neurotypicalization and inclusive universal design extend to other marginalized groups as well as to neurotypical students, and whether and how spillover benefits across minoritized identities (e.g., LGBTQ+) materialize.
abstract: Neurodivergent people experience significant disadvantages obtaining and maintaining employment. Locating our analysis at the intersection of the performativity, neurodiversity, and role of business school literatures, we argue that business schools exacerbate these issues by being designed and operated around neurotypical culture, curriculum, and teaching practices. Substantively redressing these issues and making business schools neuroinclusive requires more than the symbolic performativity that is typically the case with diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) initiatives. Instead, there is an urgent need to move to substantive performativity through a process that we call de-neurotypicalizing the business school. De-neurotypicalization involves challenging and changing the implicit neurotypical assumptions that pervade business school education and work practices. A key mechanism is applying universal design principles to both learning and work. By taking substantive actions toward de-neurotypicalization, we argue that business schools will make business education genuinely open to both neurodivergent and neurotypical people. Our analysis also advances theory on DEI in business schools by illustrating that universal design offers an inclusive solution to broader equity concerns.
apa_citation: Spoor, J. R., Jameson, T. P., Billsberry, J., & Vogus, T. J. (2026). A design for all: De-neurotypicalizing business schools and achieving substantive performativity. *Academy of Management Learning & Education*. https://doi.org/10.5465/amle.2025.0023

title: The benchmark greenium
methods: Daily security-level dynamic term-structure model (DTSM) with an additional time-varying green factor, estimated via Bayesian/MCMC techniques to jointly price German federal green and conventional ('twin') sovereign bonds and extract a time-varying benchmark greenium purged of pecuniary and non-pecuniary non-environmental factors; complemented by regression analyses linking the estimated greenium to proxies of environmental concerns.
industry: Sovereign fixed-income markets (government bonds)
time_period: September 2020 through the sample end (data beginning with the first German green bund issuance in September 2020)
units: German federal green bonds and their conventional 'twin' counterparts, plus the broader cross-section of outstanding German federal (Bund) securities
authors: D’Amico, S.; Klausmann, J.; Pancost, N. A.
theories: convenience yield theory; dynamic term structure model; no-arbitrage asset pricing; taste premium / green preferences
keywords: Dynamic no-arbitrage models; ESG; Green bonds
research_question: Is there a "taste" premium for green assets (a greenium) that stems solely from investors' environmental values rather than their cash-flow expectations, and if so, how large is it, how has it evolved over time, and how does it vary across investment horizons?
mechanism_summary: The authors extend a no-arbitrage dynamic term structure model to jointly price German federal green and conventional bonds by adding a green factor that affects only green securities. Because twin green and conventional Bunds have identical cash flows and default risk, any systematic yield differential must reflect non-cash-flow factors; the model decomposes these into pecuniary and non-pecuniary components and isolates the part driven solely by environmental concerns. The resulting benchmark greenium behaves as a convenience yield on green assets that varies with climate attitudes and perceived climate risk, generating a term structure of expected green returns that incorporates greenium risk and that can invert when short-horizon environmental concerns intensify relative to long-horizon ones.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces a benchmark greenium — a frictionless, time-varying taste premium on risk-free green assets — and shows that the observable green spread is a noisy and potentially misleading proxy for it because it bundles the greenium with the conventional convenience yield, broad market risk, and temporary demand-supply imbalances. By embedding a dedicated green factor inside a dynamic no-arbitrage term structure model, the authors deliver the first horizon-dependent, time-varying estimate of the taste premium for green assets and of its associated risk premium, extending convenience-yield theory (Krishnamurthy and Vissing-Jorgensen; Nagel) from money-like assets to environmental assets. The framework generalizes to any convenience yield that affects one group of assets but not others and supplies a benchmark for pricing riskier green securities.
practical_implication: Because the greenium is substantial, highly time-varying, and usually largest at shorter horizons (with an inverted term structure by early 2023), governments can capture a larger subsidy from climate-motivated investors by issuing shorter-term green bonds, passing the savings to taxpayers. The risk-free benchmark greenium also provides a reference price for riskier green assets, facilitating corporate green bond issuance and stimulating private funding for the green transition. Investors and portfolio managers should interpret green spreads cautiously, since they conflate environmental preferences with conventional convenience yields and market-risk shocks.
limitations: The analysis is confined to the German sovereign 'twin' bond market, which offers an unusually clean setting but limits generalizability to other issuers, currencies, and credit-risky green bonds. The greenium is identified through a parametric DTSM whose assumptions about latent factor dynamics and the green factor specification may influence its estimated level and term structure.
future_research: The framework could be applied to other convenience yields affecting distinct asset groups, and to riskier green securities (corporate green bonds, green sovereigns with default risk) once a benchmark greenium is available as a reference. Future work could also link time variation in the greenium more tightly to measurable climate policy shocks, investor flows, and climate-risk news, and extend the model to additional sovereign twin markets as more green bonds are issued.
abstract: While the green spread correlates with stock market prices, the conventional convenience yield, and temporary demand-supply imbalances, our greenium correlates only with proxies of environmental concerns.
apa_citation: D’Amico, S., Klausmann, J., & Pancost, N. A. (2026). The benchmark greenium. *Journal of Financial Economics*, 176, 104217. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jfineco.2025.104217

title: Scale, scope, speed, and customization (pick two): Resourcing drives trade-offs in local compassion venturing effectiveness
methods: Conceptual theory-building paper that develops a resourcing framework of local compassion venturing effectiveness; integrates the psychology literature on scarcity with research on entrepreneurial resourcefulness and effectiveness to derive four propositions linking two resourcing perspectives (personal resourcing mindset x social resourcing orientation) to trade-offs among four dimensions of response effectiveness (scale, scope, speed, customization)
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
authors: Seyb, S.; Williams, T. A.; Shepherd, D. A.
theories: compassion organizing; entrepreneurial resourcefulness; scarcity theory
keywords: adverse events; compassion venturing; effectiveness; local entrepreneurship; social impact
research_question: When local individuals launch new ventures to alleviate suffering after an adverse event, how can they navigate abrupt resource constraints to develop effective responses?
mechanism_summary: Adverse events abruptly deplete resources while simultaneously creating urgent, geographically bounded needs for entrepreneurial action. Local compassion venturers vary along two resourcing perspectives (personal mindset and social orientation), which shape whom they turn to for resources and how they deploy them. Each of the four resulting pathways privileges two of the four effectiveness dimensions at the expense of the remaining two, producing predictable trade-offs rather than convergent, uniformly effective responses. Because ventures in the same community face similar conditions, these venture-level trade-offs aggregate into community-level patterns of complementarity or cannibalization in suffering alleviation.
theoretical_contribution: The paper challenges the implicit assumption in the crisis and prosocial organizing literatures that the dimensions of response effectiveness (scale, scope, speed, customization) co-vary, and instead theorizes systematic trade-offs among them driven by heterogeneous resourcing perspectives. It also challenges the assumption that community-level suffering alleviation equals the sum of venture-level effectiveness, exposing potential cannibalization and complementarities across ventures. Finally, by showing how scarcity-insiders and scarcity-outsiders can achieve unexpected advantages in customization, speed, or scale, it elaborates the boundaries of entrepreneurial resourcefulness and reframes previously disadvantaged locals as uniquely positioned to alleviate others' suffering in specific ways.
practical_implication: Because no single compassion venture is likely to achieve all four dimensions of effectiveness simultaneously, community recovery depends on a portfolio of complementary ventures and actors whose strengths and weaknesses offset one another. Entrepreneurs, funders, NGOs, and government coordinators should clarify which effectiveness dimensions are most urgent in a given recovery phase and deliberately form partnerships that compensate for predictable deficiencies, rather than pushing every venture to maximize all four. Increasing visibility of other actors' activities and lowering coordination costs can reduce duplication and help locals situate their ventures within a broader, synergistic response.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: Future scholarship can examine the value and trade-offs of simultaneously mobilizing local embeddedness for customization while leveraging non-local pipelines for scale, including coordination costs, legitimacy or authenticity threats, and mission drift, and explore the feasibility of achieving scalable customization of responses. Researchers could also integrate the framework with humanitarian logistics and supply-chain management to study how complementary rather than duplicative efforts can be encouraged, and extend it to explore the dark side of compassion venturing (e.g., virtue washing and exploitation of brokering positions). Further work could examine how path dependence in early resourcing choices shapes ventures' longer-term viability and their role in system-level recovery.
abstract: Building on previous work explaining why some individuals respond entrepreneur­ ially in the face of an adverse event, we focus on the post-launch phase of venturing to theorize about how locals’ heterogenous resourcing approaches influence the effectiveness of their new ventures. Our resourcing framework of local compassion venturing effectiveness outlines four pathways through which locals navigate the conditions imposed by the adverse event to develop effective entrepreneurial responses. We offer four propositions explaining how locals’ varying perspectives on resourcing lead them to engage in resource acquisition and deployment in ways that produce different tradeoffs between the four formative dimensions of response effectiveness (scale, scope, speed, and customization). By explaining these tradeoffs, our theorizing contributes to the entrepreneurship literature by providing new insights into why and how compassion ventures differ in effectiveness and highlighting situations in which the aggregate effectiveness of individual ventures may not accurately reflect the community-level impact of compassion venturing.
apa_citation: Seyb, S., Williams, T. A., & Shepherd, D. A. (2026). Scale, scope, speed, and customization (pick two): Resourcing drives trade-offs in local compassion venturing effectiveness. *Journal of Business Venturing*, 41, 106580. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jbusvent.2026.106580

title: Designing Dual-Purpose Organizations: The Role of Soft Governance
methods: Conceptual / theory-building paper. The authors develop an integrative process model of dual-purpose organizing by synthesizing the organization design, hybrid organizing, and goal multiplicity literatures; they introduce and define the construct of 'soft governance' and articulate its interaction with formal design across the sorting, structuring, and socialization phases of organizing.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
authors: Szerb, A.; Kivleniece, I.; Aggarwal, V. A.
theories: goal multiplicity / multi-objective organizing; hybrid organizing; micro-foundations of organizing; organization design theory
keywords: aggregation problem; consolidation problem; coordination problems; dual-purpose organizations; goal heterogeneity; hybrid organizing; informal design mechanisms; nonhierarchical decision-making; organization design; soft governance
research_question: How can organization design—and in particular the agentic deployment of informal mechanisms (soft governance)—address the distinctive coordination challenges (an aggregation problem and a consolidation problem) that arise in dual-purpose organizations pursuing commercial and social objectives simultaneously?
mechanism_summary: DPOs are conceptualized as facing two unique coordination problems: an aggregation problem rooted in heterogeneous individual and organizational preferences, and a consolidation problem arising because dual purposes cannot be collapsed into a single meta-goal, producing nonhierarchical and opaque decision rules. Formal design mechanisms (incentives, supervisory structures, task allocation) are necessary but insufficient under these conditions. Soft governance—the intentional, agentic deployment of informal mechanisms—complements formal design by shaping the informal decision-making structure of the organization across the three organizing processes: during sorting it attracts and screens members with aligned preferences through tacit signaling; during structuring it supplies informal feedback and authority where formal rules are ambiguous; and during socialization it diffuses and converges local decision rules via peer interactions, enabling micro-level choices to cohere with macro-level dual-purpose goals.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces and develops the construct of "soft governance," defined as the deliberate, agentic deployment of informal mechanisms (informal roles, authority, rewards, and interaction structures) that shape an organization's informal decision-making structure. By extending the scope of organization design to encompass intentionally cultivated informal elements, the authors challenge the dominant assumption that informal structures merely emerge as byproducts of formal design and show that soft governance can complement formal processes to resolve coordination challenges that are endemic to dual-purpose organizing. The integrative process model bridges the hybrid-organizing, goal-multiplicity, and organization-design literatures, recasting informal structure as a "designable" component of the organization.
practical_implication: The framework offers managers of DPOs a diagnostic lens for identifying when formal mechanisms are likely to fail and a categorization of informal mechanisms that can complement or substitute for them. Achieving coordination under goal ambiguity, the authors argue, requires not just formal structure but also the strategic, agentic construction of informal scaffolding such as informal roles, recognition-based rewards, and deliberately cultivated interaction patterns. The insights may also extend to other pluralistic organizational settings, including multinational firms and nonmarket forms of organizing, where goal divergence and decision ambiguity are endemic.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The authors call for comparative empirical studies that examine the relative effectiveness of specific soft governance mechanisms—for example, when and why informal roles outperform recognition-based rewards, or how different interaction patterns affect the convergence of decision rules across levels. They further invite research into the coevolution of individual and organizational goals over time, and into how soft governance operates in other pluralistic or contested settings beyond DPOs, such as multinational firms and nonmarket organizations.
abstract: Although the phenomenon of dual-purpose organizations (DPOs)—market-based organizations that pursue both private (commercial) and broader social objectives simultaneously— has been explored extensively in prior literature, the role of organization design in addressing the distinct coordination problems inherent to DPOs has been largely overlooked. We develop a theoretical framework that conceptualizes how design considerations can influence organizational outcomes for DPOs. We identify two central coordination challenges faced by DPOs: an aggregation problem, in which heterogeneous individual preferences and organizational goals must be effectively aligned; and a consolidation problem, in which the dual-purpose nature of DPOs prevents the consolidation of goals into a single organizational meta-goal, resulting in nonhierarchical decision-making structures. We argue that formal organization design processes and mechanisms alone may fail to resolve these coordination problems, and we conceptualize the role and notion of soft governance, which we define as the agentic deployment of informal design mechanisms that shape an organization’s informal decision-making structure. Our core contribution lies in developing an integrative model of dual-purpose organizing that demonstrates how soft governance may complement formal organization design in alleviating the coordination problems inherent in DPOs, thereby supporting their stability and growth.
apa_citation: Szerb, A., Kivleniece, I., & Aggarwal, V. A. (2026). Designing dual-purpose organizations: The role of soft governance. *Academy of Management Review*, 1–22. https://doi.org/10.5465/amr.2023.0226

title: Values, risk and biodiversity-friendly action: perceived consumer effectiveness matters
methods: Cross-sectional online survey analyzed with structural equation modelling (SEM); mediation via risk perception and moderation tests of perceived consumer effectiveness; bootstrapping with 2,000 resamples for indirect effects
industry: Consumer / general population (not industry-specific)
units: Individual adult consumers aged 18-75, recruited via an online panel with quotas for gender, age, and region
authors: Uusitalo, O.; Rouhiainen, H.; Salimi, M.
theories: Perceived consumer effectiveness; Pro-environmental behaviour (PEB) framework; Schwartz value theory (egoistic, altruistic, biospheric orientations); Value-Belief-Norm theory
keywords: Biodiversity loss; Pro-environmental behaviours; Risk perception; Sustainable consumption; Value orientations
research_question: What are the antecedents of biodiversity-respectful consumer behaviours, and how do value orientations, risk perception of biodiversity loss, and perceived consumer effectiveness jointly shape environmental citizenship/activism, sustainable consumption, and biodiversity-friendly food choices?
mechanism_summary: Grounded in value-belief-norm reasoning, the study argues that stable value orientations shape consumers' cognitive appraisal of biodiversity loss as a risk, which in turn activates pro-environmental actions. Biospheric values (valuing nature intrinsically) strongly elevate risk perception, whereas altruistic values (prioritizing human others) reduce it because biodiversity loss appears distant and indirect relative to immediate human needs. Risk perception then drives three types of behavior. Perceived consumer effectiveness — the belief that one's actions can make a difference — amplifies the translation of risk perception into activism, sustainable consumption, and food choices, functioning as a self-efficacy-like moderator of agency.
theoretical_contribution: The study extends the pro-environmental behaviour literature by distinguishing biodiversity-respectful consumption from more familiar climate-oriented behaviours and showing that biospheric values, not altruistic values, are the dominant value-based driver of biodiversity loss risk consciousness. It contributes a moderation account in which perceived consumer effectiveness amplifies the link between risk perception and high-effort sustainable behaviours, advancing understanding of why value-behaviour gaps persist for indirect, hard-to-observe environmental threats. The counterintuitive negative effect of altruistic values highlights that nature must be incorporated as a distinct moral object in theories of sustainable consumption.
practical_implication: Marketing managers, public campaigners, and policymakers should frame biodiversity loss in terms of both its direct and indirect impacts on human and natural well-being and make the consumer's own potential impact salient. Communications should activate biospheric values, clearly convey the risks of biodiversity loss, and emphasise consumers' agency through active citizenship, sustainable consumption, and biodiversity-respectful food choices (e.g., plant-based, organic, or locally sourced products). Firms can use these insights to design non-greenwashed green marketing that connects product claims to concrete biodiversity outcomes.
limitations: Model fit was only marginal on some indices (e.g., TLI = 0.89), suggesting the specified pathways may omit relevant constructs. The data are cross-sectional and self-reported, limiting causal inference about value–risk–behaviour links. The Finnish sample, characterized by tradition and conformity-oriented values and relatively pro-environmental attitudes, may not generalize to populations with different cultural value profiles.
future_research: The authors call for strengthening the model by adding mediators such as moral obligation, eco-anxiety, or biodiversity knowledge, and moderators such as social influence, cultural factors, or personality traits. Longitudinal and experimental designs are needed to establish causality and test specific interventions, and subgroup analyses across demographics and awareness levels would test robustness. Future work should also disaggregate egoistic values (hedonic vs. financial) and altruistic values (local vs. global) and experiment with framing biodiversity loss as a personal or community issue; comparative cross-country studies involving different value orientations are also encouraged.
abstract:
Purpose – Exploiting natural resources, including the escalating production and consumption of goods, significantly impacts the environment, manifesting in crises such as biodiversity loss. This paper aims to examine the antecedents of biodiversity-respectful consumer behaviours and provide novel insights for marketing about how to promote biodiversity-respectful consumer behaviour.
Design/methodology/approach – Using structural equation modelling, this study examines the relationships among value orientations, risk perception of biodiversity loss, perceived consumer effectiveness and three types of pro-environmental behaviours, based on a survey of 1,000 Finnish consumers.
Findings – Results indicate a weak positive link between egoistic values and risk perception of biodiversity loss, while altruistic values exhibited a negative association. Biospheric values showed a strong positive effect on risk perception, which in turn positively influenced environmental citizenship, activism, sustainable consumption and biodiversity-friendly food choices. Additionally, perceived consumer effectiveness enhanced the relationship between risk perception and pro-environmental actions.
Practical implications – The results point to the importance of framing and communicating environmental problems in terms of their direct and indirect impacts on both human and natural well-being. It also emphasises the need for marketing managers to consider the context of biodiversity loss and leverage perceived consumer effectiveness in promoting consumer practices mitigate biodiversity loss.
Originality/value – This study highlights the critical role of biospheric values and perceived consumer effectiveness in fostering biodiversity-friendly behaviours. The results suggest that biospheric values strongly induce biodiversity loss risk consciousness, which is significantly associated with multiple types of environmentally sound and biodiversity-friendly behaviours. Altruistic values were found to be negatively related to biodiversity loss risk perception. Biodiversity-respectful consumer behaviour may involve characteristics that are distinct from the factors underlying other types of pro-environmental behaviours. Consumers may be unable to handle the connections between perceived risks and the actual outcomes in situations where the consequences of environmental destructions are indirect and difficult to observe.
apa_citation: Uusitalo, O., Rouhiainen, H., & Salimi, M. (2026). Values, risk and biodiversity-friendly action: Perceived consumer effectiveness matters. *Journal of Consumer Marketing*, 43(1), 108-121. https://doi.org/10.1108/JCM-01-2025-7548

title: Ecosystem initiation: a key policy challenge for circular economy transition
methods: Multiple case study (six circular ecosystem cases across Australia and the Netherlands); 19 semi-structured interviews with key informants across ecosystem functional groups, supplemented by secondary data (websites, media posts, reports); deductive thematic coding in NVivo 14 against a synthesized seven-function circular ecosystem framework.
industry: Hospitality (hotels, commercial white linen); recreational parkland/light industrial manufacturing; industrial materials exchange; retail textiles/denim; road construction/asphalt polymers
units: Circular economy ecosystems (six cases); interviewees drawn from local government, businesses, knowledge providers, suppliers, and consumers
authors: Vogel, R.; Geissdoerfer, M.
theories: business ecosystems (Moore; Adner; Baldwin et al.); circular economy; ecosystems theory
keywords: Circular economy; Circular economy transitions; Circular ecosystems; Industrial ecology; Local government
research_question: Why and how might local government initiate and orchestrate a circular economy ecosystem?
mechanism_summary: Local government, acting through empowered officers, can serve as initiator, orchestrator, and context-specific participant in a circular economy ecosystem. By convening autonomous but interdependent stakeholders around a focal CE value proposition, local government helps establish the market-sector conditions (innovation, knowledge diffusion, supply structure, regulatory support, cultural legitimacy, and demand) required for circular practices to scale. The mechanism works when actors in each of the seven functions cooperate and co-create value, and when dominant values, norms and culture shift to support circularity; absent such agency and cultural alignment, CE initiatives remain confined to niche markets.
theoretical_contribution: The paper advances ecosystem theory for the circular economy on two fronts. First, it identifies that local government has the agency to initiate and orchestrate circular ecosystems and may take up other context-specific functional roles beyond regulation. Second, it proposes a novel circular ecosystem framework with seven functions as the organizing principle (rather than stakeholders/actors), categorizing business and technology, social and behavioral, and policy and regulation perspectives, and introducing "dominant values, norms and culture" as an explicit function — offering a more nuanced conceptual and definitional basis for analyzing circular ecosystems.
practical_implication: For local governments, the guidance framework clarifies the range of roles — initiator, orchestrator, knowledge partner, procurer, cultural advocate — available to facilitate a CE transition within their jurisdictions. For firms, investors, and civil-society actors, the framework shows how to purposefully assume or shift between ecosystem roles to maximize collective value creation, de-risk investment, and capture first-mover advantages in emerging circular economy sectors.
limitations: The findings are based on a parsimonious set of case studies, all of which are mature circular ecosystems with successful outcomes, introducing a survivorship bias. Although the authors purposefully sought diversity across market sectors, the selection is limited to two developed economies (Australia and the Netherlands) and to the specific sectors sampled, constraining transferability of the findings.
future_research: The authors recommend (1) investigating circular ecosystems that have failed and the reasons for failure; (2) examining which market sectors are best suited for local government to establish circular ecosystems; and (3) testing the proposed analytical framework — particularly whether foregrounding societally dominant values, norms, and culture usefully explains circular ecosystem dynamics — through longitudinal case studies and agent-based modelling.
abstract: The circular economy (CE) is increasingly viewed as a key pathway to sustainable development, but its uptake remains behind expectations. We explore the concept of circular economy ecosystems to advance our understanding of the market sector conditions necessary for the adoption of circular economy practices by producers, consumers and other stakeholders within an ecosystem. Our methodology is a multiple case study with data collection through semi-structured qualitative interviews of key informants instrumental in the initiation of the ecosystem cases, enriched by secondary data. We identify the special conditions necessary for establishing a circular economy ecosystem and the implications for stakeholders, with a special focus on local government. We deduct a novel framework to analyse and understand the agency involved in a circular economy ecosystem. Our framework depicts seven functions with actors from each function potentially involved in the circular ecosystem. Finally, the article outlines the limitations of our work, and testable propositions and methodological avenues for longitudinal case studies, and agent-based modelling to empirically validate and refine the proposed concepts, advancing the research frontier on circular ecosystems.
apa_citation: Vogel, R., & Geissdoerfer, M. (2026). Ecosystem initiation: A key policy challenge for circular economy transition. *Journal of Industrial Ecology*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s44498-026-00002-6

title: The power of consumer solidarity framing in scaling up social impact
methods: Single exemplary qualitative case study of the French social venture C'est qui le patron?! (CQLP), 2016-2023; inductive process analysis with dual-coding by outsider authors, triangulating 21 founder media interviews, 1.6K Facebook posts with 30K comments, 54 TV/radio reports, 2,736 pages of parliamentary records, 1,823 min of nonparticipant observation, 103 emails, and three semi-structured interviews.
industry: Agri-food / dairy (fair-priced milk consumer co-operative)
units: One social venture (CQLP) and its ecosystem actors (consumers/community, media, retailers, policymakers)
n_sample: 1 focal venture; data includes 21 founder interviews, 1.6K Facebook posts, 54 media reports, 2736 pages of parliamentary transcripts, 3 semi-structured interviews
authors: Snihur, Y.; Bluntz, C.; Bocken, N.
theories: business ecosystems; collective emotional energy; entrepreneurial framing; social movement framing (diagnostic/prognostic/motivational)
keywords: Ecosystem actors; Energized solidarity framing; Scaling up; Social entrepreneurship; Social impact
research_question: How do social entrepreneurs use entrepreneurial framing to mobilize ecosystem actors to scale up social impact?
mechanism_summary: The paper develops a process model in which an entrepreneur's energized solidarity framing — articulating a societal problem (diagnostic), a collaborative consumer-based solution (prognostic), and a motivational call to fairness — infuses a mundane purchase with shared moral meaning. Positive and negative emotional energizing generates collective momentum first within the community, which is then amplified by the media, engaged by market actors seeking legitimacy, and taken up by policymakers. Reinforcing feedback loops between entrepreneur, community, media, market actors, and politicians sustain engagement over time and drive nationwide scaling outside the venture's direct control.
theoretical_contribution: The paper contributes to research on scaling up social impact by theorizing a distributed process in which a social entrepreneur's framing of a solution is progressively taken up and energized by heterogeneous ecosystem actors, enabling nationwide impact under resource scarcity. It also advances entrepreneurial framing research by introducing a social-emotional perspective — showing how shared emotions and energized solidarity framing, not cognition alone, mobilize diverse ecosystem actors and enable change.
practical_implication: Rather than confronting incumbents NGO-style, social entrepreneurs can combine consumer-solidarity content with emotional energizing to maneuver between community, media, retailers, and policymakers, adopting fair pricing even in price-competitive sectors. Skillful framing that avoids direct attacks and cultivates both social and traditional media engagement can help small ventures trigger systemic change, as illustrated by cases such as Fairphone, Fairafric, and Yuka.
limitations: The findings rest on a single exemplary case (CQLP) in France, so the distributed scaling-up process may unfold differently in other settings. Boundary conditions include the publicized nature of the French milk crisis, the concreteness of milk as a consumer product, and founder Nicolas Chabanne's skill with media and willingness to cooperate with incumbents; framing may be less effective where crises are not publicized, media is state-controlled, or founders lack such skills.
future_research: More work is needed on cognitive and emotional dynamics around social and environmental problems, including conditions under which emotional energy dissipates rather than accumulates. Comparative and cross-national studies could examine how energized framing interacts with deep versus up scaling, whether it transfers across product categories, and how authenticity is preserved when powerful actors co-opt the frame. Future research could also build on identity-based typologies to ask which social entrepreneurs are most likely to pursue distributed scaling processes.
abstract: This distributed process of scaling up social impact unfolds outside the venture's direct control, relying on community enactment as well as engagement and energizing from the media, incumbent retailers and competitors, as well as politicians. We contribute to research on scaling up social impact by explaining how and why entrepreneurial framing can mobilize diverse ecosystem actors. We also advance entrepreneurial framing research by offering a social-emotional explanation of how framing can enable change.
apa_citation: Snihur, Y., Bluntz, C., & Bocken, N. (2026). The power of consumer solidarity framing in scaling up social impact. *Journal of Business Venturing*, *41*, 106579. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jbusvent.2026.106579

title: Memento Mori: mortality salience, legacy and time perspective in sustainable consumption
methods: Cross-sectional online survey; moderated mediation analysis using Hayes' PROCESS macro Model 9 with bootstrap confidence intervals (5,000 resamples)
industry: General consumers (online survey panel)
units: Adults aged over 25
authors: Bae, H.; Jo, S. H.
theories: socioemotional selectivity theory; terror management theory
keywords: Future time perspective; Mortality legacy; Mortality salience; Socioemotional selectivity theory; Sustainable consumption; Terror management theory
research_question: How does mortality salience influence sustainable consumption behavior, and do mortality legacy (as mediator) and limited future time perspective and chronological age (as moderators) shape this relationship, drawing on terror management theory and socioemotional selectivity theory?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on terror management theory, the authors argue that awareness of death prompts individuals to manage existential anxiety through distal defenses such as upholding cultural worldviews and boosting self-esteem. In contemporary society, sustainability functions as a dominant moral worldview, so mortality salience heightens consumers' desire to leave a mortality legacy — a lasting positive contribution that transcends physical death — which in turn channels behavior into sustainable consumption as a form of symbolic immortality. Integrating socioemotional selectivity theory, the authors further theorize that consumers with a limited future time perspective perceive time as scarce and therefore become more motivated to engage in generative, legacy-oriented behaviors, strengthening the mediated path from mortality salience to sustainable consumption via mortality legacy. Chronological age was hypothesized to operate similarly but was not supported empirically.
theoretical_contribution: The study extends terror management theory by demonstrating a novel, constructive distal defense: people adopt sustainable consumption behavior, via the desire for a mortality legacy, as a way to manage death-related anxiety and achieve symbolic immortality. It contributes to the literature on antecedents of sustainable consumption by identifying mortality salience and legacy motivation as psychological drivers, and it advances socioemotional selectivity theory by showing that limited future time perspective — but not chronological age — moderates the indirect effect, suggesting that psychological rather than chronological markers of time horizon matter most in mortality-driven sustainable consumption.
practical_implication: Marketers and policymakers can promote sustainable consumption by designing communications that subtly elicit consumers' awareness of mortality and foster a desire to leave a lasting legacy, framing sustainability around "protecting what matters for future generations" or "leaving a meaningful mark" rather than using explicit fear-based appeals. Because age did not moderate the effect, such interventions can be deployed broadly without age-specific tailoring, though they should be implemented ethically and transparently. Managers should also consider consumers' future time perspective when deploying mortality salience appeals, since legacy motivations are strongest among those who perceive their time as limited.
limitations: The survey data were collected only in the USA, which may limit generalizability, especially given that consumer responses to mortality salience, mortality legacy, and sustainable consumption may differ between Asian and Western cultures. The study did not empirically compare the relative strength of opposing psychological mechanisms — helplessness or avoidance versus legacy motives — that mortality salience can elicit. The lack of a significant age moderation effect also invites reconsideration of how chronological age operates within these processes.
future_research: Future research should examine whether mortality salience similarly affects sustainable consumption behavior in Asian cultural contexts (e.g., South Korea, China, Japan) where religious traditions such as Buddhism may shape death-related cognition. Researchers should explore alternative moderating variables such as cultural values, personal environmental attitudes, and socioeconomic status, and investigate psychological markers of age (e.g., subjective, felt, or cognitive age) rather than chronological age. Further work should also examine boundary conditions under which helplessness versus legacy motives dominate as responses to mortality salience.
abstract: Purpose – The purpose of this study is to advance the understanding of the interplay between mortality salience, mortality legacy and sustainable consumption behavior. Design/methodology/approach – Data were collected through an online questionnaire from 1,000 consumers aged over 25 in the USA. Findings – The results show that mortality salience induces sustainable behavior and that mortality legacy mediates this relationship. Moreover, a limited future time perspective is found to moderate the mediation process in the path from mortality salience to mortality legacy. Furthermore, age did not emerge as a significant moderator of the indirect effect of mortality salience on sustainable consumption behavior through mortality legacy. Originality/value – The findings connect the terror management theory with the socioemotional selectivity theory. They also reveal that marketers can nudge consumers’ sustainable consumption behavior by incorporating mortality salience and legacy appeals into their marketing communications.
apa_citation: Bae, H., & Jo, S. H. (2025). Memento mori: Mortality salience, legacy and time perspective in sustainable consumption. *Journal of Consumer Marketing*. https://doi.org/10.1108/JCM-09-2025-8257

title: 2025 Presidential Address: Activism, Impact and Science: Management Scholarship in a Challenging Era
methods: Editorial essay (AOM Presidential Address); reflective argumentation with illustrative references to prior research and AOM initiatives
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
authors: Bamberger, P.
theories: engaged scholarship
keywords: academic activism; academic freedom; business school accreditation; engaged scholarship; global inclusion; open science; rigor and relevance; scholar-practitioner gap; scholarly impact; scientific integrity
research_question: Not reported in paper
mechanism_summary: Bamberger advances a normative argument rather than a causal model. He contends that management scholarship can have impact only if it is relevant, but that relevance must be pursued without sacrificing rigor or scientific credibility. He argues that the payoff structures governing scholarship (citation-based metrics, high-impact journal counts, and ranking systems) incentivize questionable research practices and crowd out socially useful work; that academic activism, while understandable, can undermine open science and fuel anti-intellectualism when advocacy shapes design and execution; and that engaged scholarship — agenda-setting, globally inclusive, and communicated accessibly to practitioners and publics — is the route by which management science can simultaneously enhance rigor, relevance, and credibility.
theoretical_contribution: The address synthesizes three AOM-led initiatives into a programmatic vision for the field: (a) reforming the definition and measurement of scholarly impact through an AACSB-led coalition aimed at updating accreditation and ranking criteria to reward evidence-based real-world impact; (b) reaffirming academic freedom alongside open science and scientific integrity, with explicit warnings about how advocacy-driven research erodes credibility; and (c) building bridges for global relevance by extending AOM's infrastructure to underrepresented regions and by translating research for practitioner and public audiences. Bamberger frames these as components of Van de Ven's engaged scholarship, arguing that this stance is how the field can "set the agenda" on issues such as human-capital sustainability, business as a vehicle for peace and development, and redesigning educational organizations.
practical_implication: Business school deans, journal editors, publishers, and tenure committees should adjust incentive structures so that evidence-based, real-world impact and diverse forms of contribution — not just theoretical novelty or high-impact-journal placement — are recognized and rewarded. Scholars studying politically loaded topics (e.g., DEI, pay transparency, inequality) should keep advocacy out of research design and execution and report findings fully, including inconvenient ones. The field should invest in globally inclusive infrastructure (memberships, workshops, open-access outlets, rotating meetings) and in practitioner-accessible communication channels such as AOM Today so that research can reach and inform decision-makers.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: Bamberger calls on the field to move from reactive to agenda-setting research, including work that: (a) broadens sustainability research to encompass human-capital sustainability and work-based interventions; (b) examines how business organizations can serve as vehicles for sustainable economic development and regional conflict resolution; and (c) develops and tests new models of educational organizations to replace industrial-scale public schooling grounded in scientific management.
abstract: 2025 PRESIDENTIAL ADDRESS: ACTIVISM, IMPACT AND SCIENCE: MANAGEMENT SCHOLARSHIP IN A CHALLENGING ERA PETER BAMBERGER Tel Aviv University
apa_citation: Bamberger, P. (2025). 2025 Presidential Address: Activism, impact and science: Management scholarship in a challenging era. *Academy of Management Review*, 1-5. https://doi.org/10.5465/amr.2025.0742

title: Englishization Beyond the Global North: Dependency, Struggle, and Resistance in Brazilian Management Research
methods: Qualitative study; 55 semi-structured interviews with Brazilian management academics and journal editors conducted in three waves (2020-2023); abductive analysis with constant comparison, line-by-line coding, and axial coding
industry: Higher education / management academia
units: Management academics (young and senior researchers) and journal editors in Brazilian management academia
authors: Boussebaa, M.; Alcadipani, R.; Vianna, F.; Pina e Cunha, M.; Clegg, S.
theories: dependency theory; postcolonial theory
keywords: Brazil; Englishization; Frankenstein papers; Global North; Global South; academic dependency; counter-hegemonic resistance; dependency theory; management research; postcolonial ambivalence; postcolonial theory; strategic compliance
research_question: How are imperatives to publish in English experienced within Brazilian management academia, and how do dynamics of dependency and postcoloniality shape these experiences?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on dependency theory and postcolonial theory, the paper theorizes Englishization as a mechanism of academic dependency: global Anglophone hierarchies and rankings pressure Global South research systems to align with Northern epistemic norms, and these pressures are amplified domestically by Brazilian journals and evaluation systems that internalize Anglo-American standards in pursuit of international visibility. Within this structural terrain, materially constrained academics engage in hybrid, ambivalent practices—strategic compliance, mimicry "without mastery," postcolonial ambivalence, and counter-hegemonic resistance—that both reproduce and destabilize the dominance of English. Englishization is thus enacted as a contested, uneven process shaped by the interplay of Global North–South inequalities, domestic institutional logics, and constrained local agency.
theoretical_contribution: The study offers a more nuanced, context-sensitive, and theoretically-informed account of Englishization by (1) challenging the assumption that Englishization unfolds uniformly across national contexts and foregrounding intra-national institutional variation (elite management schools vs. public universities); (2) conceptualizing Englishization as both reflective and constitutive of academic dependency, reproduced through domestic institutions' strategic appropriation of Anglo-American norms under structural constraint; and (3) demonstrating that Englishization is actively negotiated through hybrid practices—strategic compliance, postcolonial ambivalence, and counter-hegemonic resistance—rather than being simply adopted or resisted. By bridging dependency and postcolonial theory, the paper advances a politically grounded understanding of Englishization as entangled with enduring Global North–South inequalities.
practical_implication: Business schools, journal editors, and research-evaluation bodies in the Global South should recognize that imposing English-language publication requirements without supporting linguistic and material resources can produce poorly translated "Frankenstein" papers that undermine rather than enhance international visibility. Domestic evaluation systems (e.g., Qualis, SciELO) and local journals should reconsider policies that reward English-language publication as a proxy for quality, and support linguistic diversity and Portuguese-language scholarship as legitimate venues. More broadly, decolonizing management knowledge production requires attending to the intra-national inequalities and material constraints that shape whose scholarship becomes visible in global academic circuits.
limitations: Findings from Brazil may not generalize to other Global South contexts with different colonial histories, economic structures, and academic traditions. The study focused on academics and journal editors but paid limited attention to subfields or subdisciplines within management, and its interpretive qualitative design limits broad generalizability and may reflect self-reporting biases.
future_research: Future studies should investigate Englishization in other Global South countries (e.g., China, former British and French colonies) and use comparative cross-national designs to examine how different colonial legacies and academic traditions shape dependency, struggle, and resistance. Research should also explore disciplinary variation in Englishization within Brazilian and other Global South contexts, use quantitative methods to systematically examine patterns of Englishization and its impact on visibility, and extend analysis to teaching and service domains of academic work.
abstract: This paper advances understanding of Englishization in management research by addressing a key limitation in the existing literature: its predominant focus on the Global North, especially Europe. Drawing on dependency theory and postcolonial theory, we examine how management academics experience and navigate Englishization in Brazil. Based on qualitative research, we found that Brazilian academics, like their European counterparts, faced growing pressures to publish in international journals, effectively compelling publication in English, though such expectations were largely concentrated in elite institutions. We also found that Brazilian journals themselves increasingly adopted English in pursuit of international visibility, thereby intensifying pressure to publish in English and extending Englishization beyond the elite. One consequence was the publication of “Frankenstein” papers—the result of limited English proficiency and financial constraints—which paradoxically risked undermining the visibility Brazilian journals sought to enhance. Yet Englishization was not passively accepted: it was actively negotiated through strategic compliance, postcolonial ambivalence, and counter-hegemonic resistance. These findings contribute to a more nuanced, context-sensitive, and theoretically-informed understanding of Englishization by applying postcolonial and dependency perspectives to highlight its uneven trajectory across and within national contexts and to show how it is entangled with enduring Global North–South inequalities.
apa_citation: Boussebaa, M., Alcadipani, R., Vianna, F., Pina e Cunha, M., & Clegg, S. (2025). Englishization beyond the Global North: Dependency, struggle, and resistance in Brazilian management research. *Academy of Management Learning & Education*. https://doi.org/10.5465/amle.2023.0245

title: Firm Growth and Corporate Social Responsibility
methods: Panel data analysis; OLS regressions with firm and year fixed effects; instrumental-variable two-stage models and ITCV tests for endogeneity; moderation analyses
industry: Multi-industry (firms listed in LSEG ESG database)
country: 46 countries (global sample spanning shareholder- and stakeholder-oriented economies)
units: Firm-year observations
authors: DesJardine, M. R.; Kim, J.; Bansal, P.
theories: attention-based view of the firm; resource-based trade-offs
keywords: business sustainability; corporate social responsibility; financial slack; firm growth; managerial attention
research_question: How does the rate of firm growth affect firms' CSR engagement, and how is this relationship shaped by firms' financial and attentional resources?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on the attention-based view of the firm, the authors argue that managerial attention and financial capital are finite resources that both firm growth and CSR draw upon. When growth accelerates, managers must allocate scarce cognitive and financial resources toward coordinating expansion, scaling operations, and meeting short-term performance targets, leaving less bandwidth and liquidity for CSR initiatives that yield intangible, long-term, and difficult-to-measure returns. Because CSR outcomes are distant and peripheral to core business activity, managers under pressure to deliver measurable growth-related outcomes reallocate attention and resources away from CSR. Performance variability amplifies this trade-off by further depleting attentional reservoirs, while financial slack attenuates it by providing a capital cushion that preserves managerial latitude to pursue non-growth initiatives during periods of rapid expansion.
theoretical_contribution: The study advances CSR research by identifying firm growth—one of the most pervasive and central features of organizational life—as a previously underexplored driver of CSR engagement, complementing work on organizational architecture, identity, and institutional drivers. It integrates the attention-based view with CSR theory to foreground the role of managerial attention (not only financial slack) as a scarce input that shapes corporate commitments, and it links the attention-based view with stakeholder governance by showing how growth pressures condition the shareholder-stakeholder trade-off. In doing so, the paper reframes the growth-sustainability debate away from a purely ideological contest and toward a pragmatic, trade-off-based account of managerial resource allocation.
practical_implication: For companies committed to CSR, the authors recommend establishing systems and controls that insulate CSR from fluctuations in managerial attention and financial resources—for instance, CSR departments with independent budgets (via corporate foundations or trusts) and strategic autonomy, analogous to governance structures afforded to R&D. Elevating CSR to an executive leadership position can help integrate it into the firm's core rather than treating it as peripheral. Socially responsible shareholders and stakeholders can also counteract growth pressures by putting forth CSR-related proposals during high-growth periods to redirect managerial attention and resources back toward CSR.
limitations: The study does not directly observe the microlevel managerial processes that link growth rates to CSR decisions, since attention and resource-allocation decisions are hard to capture with archival data. The analysis also takes a relatively static snapshot and is agnostic about the long-run and overall effect of growth on CSR and business sustainability, which may differ from the short-term trade-offs documented.
future_research: Future work should directly observe managers in high-growth firms (e.g., through qualitative or field methods) to examine whether growth-CSR trade-offs are made deliberately or emerge unintentionally. Researchers should also study the longer-term, historical relationship between growth and CSR, including whether growth-focused firms eventually produce game-changing CSR innovations that offset earlier trade-offs. Further theorizing is needed on how managers make attention-related trade-offs in pursuing CSR and on which organizational structures and characteristics shape those decisions.
abstract: This paper examines how the rate of firm growth influences corporate social responsibility (CSR). Periods of rapid growth can place substantial financial and managerial demands on firms, requiring leaders to prioritize the allocation of limited resources toward managing expansion. We propose that when firms grow quickly, managers may limit CSR investments—not because they undervalue CSR, but because growth itself consumes financial and attentional capacity. Using 19 years of data from 4,305 firms across 46 countries, we find that faster firm growth is associated with lower levels of CSR activity. This relationship is moderated by firms’ financial and attentional resources: firms with greater financial slack and managerial bandwidth maintain stronger CSR engagement even while growing rapidly. Overall, our results suggest that the relationship between growth and CSR reflects a pragmatic balancing of competing demands.
apa_citation: DesJardine, M. R., Kim, J., & Bansal, P. (2025). Firm growth and corporate social responsibility. *Journal of Management*. https://doi.org/10.1177/01492063251398785
