title: The Need for Speed: The Impact of Capital Constraints on Strategic Misconduct
methods: Empirical quantitative analysis using ordinary least squares (OLS), instrumental variable (IV), and coarsened exact matching models on vehicle-level panel data; complemented by abductive qualitative fieldwork including 60+ hours of interviews and a survey of 100 firm owners; leverages a 2017 industry-wide strike in South Africa as a source of quasi-exogenous variation in interest rates
industry: Minibus taxi industry (informal public transport / SMEs)
time_period: Vehicles financed January 2016 to December 2018; outcomes measured over first 15 months of operation
units: Individual minibus taxi vehicles (Toyota 16-seater Quantums) financed by a large South African taxi financier, with GPS telemetry, financing, owner, and insurance-claim data
n_sample: 3,395 vehicle observations in the main regression sample (from 4,407 unique vehicles and ~8 million daily vehicle observations across 7,593 financed vehicles)
authors: Eaglin, F. C.
theories: financial constraints and misconduct; institutional voids; strategic misconduct
keywords: capital constraints; decision making; emerging markets; firm performance; minibus taxi industry; misconduct; organizational studies; small and medium-sized enterprises; strategic misconduct; strategy
research_question: What is the impact of relaxing capital constraints on strategic misconduct among small and medium-sized firms in emerging markets whose survival and profitability depend on misconduct, and why do such firms engage in strategic misconduct even when it carries high risks?
mechanism_summary: High capital costs create high minimum revenue requirements to stay in business; taxi operators speed and drive aggressively to complete as many trips as possible to meet loan obligations and avoid default, whose penalties in this setting extend beyond asset loss to loss of livelihood for extended families, loss of standing in associations, and risk of violence. When interest rates fall, the required monthly revenue drops, reducing the marginal benefit of the most dangerous forms of misconduct; firms therefore cut back on the most extreme speeding and collisions while continuing milder speeding. The mechanism is thus one in which relaxing capital constraints loosens the survival pressure that makes severe strategic misconduct a dominant strategy choice for SMEs operating under intense competition, weak regulation, and institutional voids.
theoretical_contribution: The paper argues that strategic misconduct is a core choice among strategic options for firms facing severe financial pressure, not merely a product of moral failing or perverse incentives, and shows that relaxing capital constraints can reduce the most severe misconduct with broad public-welfare benefits. It contributes to the literatures on misconduct, entrepreneurship in emerging markets, institutional voids, and prosocial firm behavior by studying survival-oriented SMEs rather than large well-resourced firms or individual financial advisors, and by linking strategic misconduct directly to weak regulatory environments and high capital costs. It also suggests that mainstream management strategy theories may have limited applicability for resource-constrained firms operating under intense competition and weak regulation, and that previous estimates of the social cost of expensive capital understate its true welfare impact once misconduct is accounted for.
practical_implication: For financiers serving SMEs in competitive environments with weak regulation, charging excessively high interest rates harms both the lender and the borrower: lower rates improve loan performance, reduce accidents involving third parties, and protect employees and the broader public by lowering the incidence of the most extreme strategic misconduct. Policymakers and funders should therefore consider how to deliver more affordable capital to such firms, for example through working-capital facilities or interest-rate caps, and should attend to the outsized penalties of default that push owners toward dangerous behavior. Improved measurement of misconduct in SME settings can aid the design of better markets for capital and services.
limitations: Firms are not randomly assigned lower interest rates, so selection could bias estimates upward, though OLS, IV, and matching models all yield consistent results and fieldwork suggests selection operates primarily at the point of joining an association rather than at financing. Generalizability is limited because the sample is drawn from a highly competitive, sparsely regulated, and often violent transport setting; effects may be more muted in settings with stronger enforcement or cheaper capital. The study does not evaluate intrafirm dynamics (e.g., owner–driver principal–agent problems) because of data limitations.
future_research: Future research should examine how differing enforcement norms and regulatory regimes mitigate or magnify the responsiveness of misconduct to capital constraints, and whether the findings extend beyond informal transport markets to other SME-intensive industries in emerging economies. Scholars should investigate intrafirm dynamics underlying strategic misconduct, including monitoring technologies, and explore how policymakers and funders can design financing products (e.g., working-capital facilities, lower interest rates) that reduce the penalties of default and the incentives to engage in misconduct. Further work is needed on improved measurement of misconduct in SME settings and on how such measurement can inform the design of better markets for capital and services.
abstract: Abstract. Existing literature suggests that firms engage in misconduct in response to Revised: June 3, 2024; August 5, 2024; financial pressure. However, the extent and severity of misconduct have been challenging November 14, 2024 to determine given the lack of granular data. This limitation is particularly true for smaller Accepted: November 18, 2024 firms in emerging markets whose profitability and survival depend on misconduct. This Published Online in Articles in Advance: study analyzes the impact of reducing capital constraints on misconduct using highJuly 1, 2025 frequency global positioning system and financing data from the minibus taxi industry in https://doi.org/10.1287/mnsc.2023.01957 South Africa. I find that lower interest rates, a proxy for lower capital constraints, decrease the most severe forms of misconduct measured by the propensity to speed and crash yet Copyright: © 2025 INFORMS leave more modest forms of misconduct unchanged. Qualitative interviews and survey data suggest that these results are attributable to firms’ efforts to avoid default. These find­ ings contribute to understanding misconduct as a firm strategy when facing financial pres­ sure and suggest that lower capital constraints might improve firm survival and public safety.

title: A cultural perspective on informal ride sharing
methods: Interpretivist qualitative design; 45 semi-structured telephone interviews analysed using Braun and Clarke's six-step thematic analysis (following Nikitas et al.'s systematised adaptation); inductive coding yielding 33 first-order codes, 12 second-order sub-themes and 4 final themes
industry: Urban mobility / informal transport
units: Lagos residents aged 18-64 (female 53.3%, male 46.6%; car owners 62.2%, non-car owners 37.7%; employed 80%, unemployed 8.8%, students 11.1%)
authors: Ehidiamen, A.; Cheetham, F.; Nikitas, A.
theories: Arnould and Rose's mutuality; Belk's theory of sharing; Marcoux's escaping the gift economy; Schwartz's theory of cultural values
keywords: Cultural values; Informal ride sharing; Ride sharing; Sharing; Sharing practices
research_question: Within a collectivist culture, what cultural sharing values influence consumers' engagement in informal ride sharing?
mechanism_summary: The authors theorise that culturally transmitted value orientations socialise individuals into shared norms about with whom and why one shares rides. In Nigeria's predominantly collectivist context, embeddedness and interdependence cultivate communal practices, group attachment and altruistic cultural morality that motivate informal ride sharing as a normative, non-transactional practice (Belk, 2010). At the same time, the obligation dimension — rooted in hierarchy and reciprocal expectations — can inhibit engagement, especially among younger consumers who prize autonomy and seek to "escape the gift economy" (Marcoux, 2009). Cultural sharing values therefore operate simultaneously as motivating, inhibiting and conflicting forces, shaping whether consumers enter informal ride sharing on the basis of pure sharing, mutuality or transactional avoidance.
theoretical_contribution: The study extends Schwartz's (2006) theory of cultural value dimensions into the sharing-economy literature by identifying four sub-dimensions of cultural sharing values — interdependence, functional value, cultural morality and obligation — that shape consumer engagement in informal ride sharing within a collectivist culture. By integrating Belk's (2010) conceptualisation of sharing, Marcoux's (2009) escaping the gift economy and Arnould and Rose's (2016) mutuality, it uncovers three key roles (motivating, inhibiting and conflicted) played by cultural sharing values, and shows that even within collectivist cultures subgroups may hold divergent values that challenge the dominant cultural narrative.
practical_implication: The findings offer guidance for policymakers and mobility planners in contexts where informal ride sharing coexists with limited formal transport. They should align transport strategies with local cultural norms, integrate informal practices as complementary solutions to gaps in formal infrastructure, and introduce community-based network structures in low-access areas. Mobility planners can scale peer-to-peer informal ride sharing through city-wide schemes targeting underserviced routes and peak periods, delivering both communal and functional value and connecting a broader pool of riders.
limitations: The sample is skewed toward younger consumers because of online recruitment and younger individuals' greater awareness of and activity in shared mobility, leaving older consumers' attitudes underexplored. The study focuses exclusively on informal ride sharing and does not examine the dynamics between informal and dynamic ride sharing within collectivist cultures. As an exploratory qualitative study, the findings provide depth but limit generalisability.
future_research: Further studies should explicitly explore older consumers' attitudes toward informal sharing practices. Future research could expand the inquiry to other sectors and contrasting cultural contexts to offer a more comprehensive understanding of sharing practices across societies. Quantitative methodologies could also be adopted to test relationships between cultural sharing values and consumer motivations for engaging in ride sharing.
abstract: Purpose – Although the literature on dynamic ride sharing has grown significantly, there remains a notable gap in understanding informal ride sharing, particularly within the context of developing countries. Informal ride sharing remains prevalent in sociocultural environments where community-level sharing thrives. The purpose of this study is to explore the cultural sharing values that influence consumers’ engagement in informal ride sharing. Design/methodology/approach – This study adopts a qualitative methodology based on semi-structured interviews and uses data-driven thematic analysis approach to analyse 45 semi-structured interviews conducted with participants who reside in Lagos, Nigeria. Findings – This study identifies four sub-dimensions and three key roles of cultural sharing values that influence consumers’ engagement in informal ride sharing. These sub-dimensions − interdependence, functional value, cultural morality and obligation are underpinned by distinct cultural value orientations. Together, they serve as motivating and inhibiting factors that shape consumer engagement. Practical implications – This study has implications for policymakers and mobility planners by emphasising the significance of informal ride sharing within specific sociocultural contexts. The findings provide consumer-focused insights that can inform the design of culturally responsive transport strategies that recognise and leverage the prevalence of informal sharing practices. Originality/value – Adopting a cultural lens, this study provides novel insight into the role of cultural sharing values and how it shapes consumers’ engagement in informal ride sharing.
apa_citation: Ehidiamen, A., Cheetham, F., & Nikitas, A. (2025). A cultural perspective on informal ride sharing. *Journal of Consumer Marketing*. https://doi.org/10.1108/JCM-01-2025-7571

title: The Ethics of Global Business, by Denis G. Arnold. Wiley Blackwell, 2023. 161 pp.
methods: Book review; narrative critique of Denis G. Arnold's The Ethics of Global Business (2023)
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
authors: Enderle, G.
theories: claim-rights theory; human rights cosmopolitanism
keywords: Base of the Pyramid; Tripartite Framework; corporate social responsibility; global business ethics; human rights; human rights cosmopolitanism; transnational corporations
research_question: Not reported in paper.
mechanism_summary: Not reported in paper.
theoretical_contribution: Not reported in paper.
practical_implication: Enderle emphasizes that Arnold's action-oriented analysis is valuable for practice: TNCs should respect the basic human rights of people impacted at the BoP, ensure minimal health and safety conditions in the workplace, inform employees of workplace hazards during hiring, and pay a weekly wage consistent with basic human dignity. Business ventures engaging Moderate and Extreme Poverty populations should pursue empowerment strategies (assessed against human rights, utility, and capabilities) rather than exploitation strategies. The book is recommended for teaching, research, and training in TNCs.
limitations: Enderle raises four critical remarks about Arnold's book: (1) it does not fully apply Gewirth's five-component structure of claim-rights, leaving imprecision about subjects, objects, respondents, binding nature, and justification of human rights; (2) it conflates "duty" and "responsibility," defining TNCs as "Duty Bearers" rather than "Bearers of Responsibility" and retaining the confusing term CSR; (3) its assessment of the Tripartite Framework is less favorable than warranted, and it omits a philosophical justification grounded in human dignity; and (4) the cited literature is almost exclusively pre-2013 and would benefit from more recent references.
future_research: Enderle suggests that the philosophical justification of human rights in pluralistic societies, the precise division of obligations between states and corporations, and the clarification of responsibility as a three-aspect ethical term (who, for what, towards whom) deserve further exploration. He also notes that the long-term impact of the Tripartite Framework—visible, for instance, in the EU's 2025 Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive—merits continued study, and that the intriguing issues raised in Arnold's book deserve to be further explored and discussed.
abstract: n his book The Ethics of Global Business (2023) Denis Arnold addresses highly important challenges of global business, with a focus on the relations of the powerful transnational corporations (TNCs) to the Base of the Pyramid (BoP)— that is, four billion people below $9.05 per day (107, Figure 7.1)—and the ethical assessment in terms of universal human rights. The author draws on decades of research and his familiarity as a five-year editor of BEQ with a vast part of literature of business ethics. He is also supported by the collaboration of Andrew Valentin and Laura Williams in the last two chapters (7 and 8).
apa_citation: Enderle, G. (2025). The ethics of global business, by Denis G. Arnold. Wiley Blackwell, 2023. 161 pp. *Business Ethics Quarterly*, 35(4), 706-709. https://doi.org/10.1017/beq.2025.10100

title: Constructing Carbon Abatement Cost Curves
methods: Analytical/conceptual model development of a generic combinatorial abatement cost framework; numerical calibration to European cement plants using industry data (ECRA 2022) with sensitivity analyses in the appendices
industry: Cement manufacturing (Portland cement), with discussion of steel, chemicals, power, and transportation
country: European Union (cement producers subject to the EU Emissions Trading System)
time_period: Calibration based on recent industry data, including 2023 average EU ETS carbon price (€85/ton)
units: European cement reference plants and their abatement lever portfolios
n_sample: Nine elementary abatement levers, yielding up to 2^9 = 512 combined levers; 18 cost-efficient combined levers identified
authors: Glenk, G.; Meier, R.; Reichelstein, S.
theories: combinatorial optimization of production decisions; life-cycle costing / cost accounting; marginal abatement cost theory
keywords: capacity investments; carbon emissions; industrial decarbonization; life-cycle costing; marginal abatement cost
research_question: How can firms construct abatement cost curves that accurately capture the cost of alternative carbon dioxide emission reduction targets when elementary abatement levers exhibit interactions, and what do such curves imply for optimal corporate decarbonization pathways under carbon pricing regimes like the EU Emissions Trading System?
mechanism_summary: The paper articulates a cost-accounting mechanism. A firm choosing among a finite set of binary elementary levers (energy efficiency, process changes, input substitutions, fuel switching, carbon capture and sequestration, etc.) faces a combinatorial optimization problem in which the Total Abatement Cost is the life-cycle expenditure of the cheapest combined lever that meets a given emission target. Because the emission and cost effects of levers interact when deployed together, the resulting MAC curve is a step function that is not necessarily monotonically increasing, and the firm's optimal response to a carbon price is the combined lever whose marginal cost crosses the prevailing carbon price. Carbon prices therefore select among only a subset of cost-efficient combined levers, and shifts in prices generate discrete jumps in optimal abatement, investment, and production cost.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces a generic combinatorial model of abatement cost curves that, in contrast to the classical McKinsey-style MAC curves, explicitly accounts for interactions between elementary levers and yields step-function MAC curves that need not be monotonically increasing. By grounding abatement cost in life-cycle costing concepts familiar from management accounting, it bridges environmental economics and cost accounting and provides a tractable framework for deriving firms' optimal abatement responses, the carbon prices that trigger specific technologies, and the minimum lump-sum payments required under carbon contracts for difference. The paper thereby contributes a formal foundation for assessing the credibility of corporate decarbonization pledges and for quantifying the pass-through of carbon prices into product costs.
practical_implication: The framework gives companies and external analysts a planning tool for projecting decarbonization pathways, identifying cost-efficient combinations of abatement levers, and estimating the carbon prices required to make particular technologies (e.g., carbon capture and sequestration) economically viable. Applied to European cement, the model shows that a €85/ton EU ETS price incentivizes roughly a one-third reduction in direct emissions, while €126/ton triggers a 78 percent reduction and €141/ton delivers near-full decarbonization at an additional levelized product cost of only about €16 per ton of cement (roughly 12 percent of the 2023 market price). The approach also allows governments to gauge the minimum lump-sum payment needed to secure bilateral carbon contracts for difference with heavy-emitting firms.
limitations: The calibration is limited to European cement production and to currently available abatement technologies, so the quantitative estimates may not generalize to other industries or future cost trajectories. The model treats the adoption of elementary levers as a static, deterministic binary choice and abstracts from uncertainty in carbon prices and learning-driven improvements in abatement technologies. Residual emissions projections are contingent on current manufacturing and abatement technologies, and the authors note that such forecasts must be qualified by that reference point.
future_research: A promising extension is to model lever adoption as a stochastic, dynamic decision problem that incorporates uncertain learning effects for abatement technologies and volatile carbon prices, allowing firms to value the option of waiting before investing. The framework can also be extended to quantify the effects of alternative accounting rules for CO2 emissions, including combinations of biomass with carbon capture that could yield net-negative cement production. Finally, the authors encourage applying the model to other hard-to-decarbonize, essential industries such as steel, glass, and chemicals.
abstract: Companies across industries face increasing pressure to assess the costs of decarbonizing their operations. This paper develops a generic model for constructing abatement cost curves in connection with carbon dioxide emissions. The resulting abatement cost curves provide a planning tool for companies seeking to project their decarbonization pathways and to determine optimal abatement levels in response to environmental regulations such as carbon pricing. We calibrate our model in the context of European cement producers that are required to obtain emission permits under the European Emissions Trading System. We find that a price of e85 per ton of carbon dioxide, as observed on average in 2023, incentivizes firms to reduce their annual direct emissions by about one-third relative to the status quo. Yet, this incentive increases sharply when prices rise above the benchmark of e100 per ton of carbon dioxide.
apa_citation: Glenk, G., Meier, R., & Reichelstein, S. (2025). Constructing carbon abatement cost curves. *The Accounting Review*. https://doi.org/10.2308/TAR-2025-0315

title: Magic Will Happen if You Gather Us in One Room: Gender Homophily and Women-Only Networking
methods: Qualitative inductive study combining participant observation, semi-structured interviews, and secondary document analysis; triangulated across data sources with iterative coding to derive meanings of gender homophily and distinguish enforced from agentic homophily.
industry: Male-dominated, competitive industries including banking, finance, corporate law, consulting, media, tech start-ups, pharma, energy, and advertising
country: Primarily Germany and the United Kingdom; participants from multiple countries including Poland, United States, Brazil, China, Kenya, the Netherlands, and Switzerland
units: Women attending corporate-sponsored women-only networking events (junior, mid-career, and senior-level professionals)
n_sample: 40 observed events (120 hours); 75 interviews; 49 informal conversations; 10 member checks; 150 secondary documents
authors: Hein, P.; Diehl, M.-R.; Kreutzer, K.
theories: activist choice homophily; benevolent sexism; gender homophily; gendered organizations theory
keywords: gender homophily; gendered organizations; inequality; meanings; networking; women
research_question: How do women perceive and experience networking at women-only events, and how do contextual factors shape the meanings women attach to gender homophily in such settings?
mechanism_summary: Women internalize gendered organizational structures differently depending on their seniority, which shapes the meanings (status-driven for senior-level, instrumental for mid-career, expressive for junior-level) they attach to networking with other women. When events are designed with enforced homophily — a patronizing, benevolent-sexism frame that treats women as a homogeneous disadvantaged group needing help — these differences in meanings become salient, fostering distancing and preventing women from leveraging homophilic ties. When events permit agentic homophily — granting women autonomy to shape the format and pursue professional outcomes — the differences in meanings recede, enabling collective, business-focused networking that can challenge rather than reproduce gendered organizational structures.
theoretical_contribution: The study reconceptualizes gender homophily as a multifaceted force shaped by the meanings individuals attach to it and by the context in which networking unfolds, rather than as a singular social mechanism. By distinguishing enforced from agentic homophily and uncovering three interlinked meanings (status-driven, instrumental, expressive), the paper extends research on gender homophily, activist choice homophily, and gendered organizations, showing that well-intentioned corporate women-only events can reproduce benevolent sexism while agentic settings enable women to challenge gendered structures.
practical_implication: Organizers should recognize that women-only networking events designed with a patronizing, "fix-the-women" framing can perpetuate benevolent sexism and structural barriers rather than dismantle them. Companies should grant women agency to shape events from the ground up, focus on collective professional outcomes, de-emphasize seniority hierarchies, and consider mixed-gender formats that recognize women's achievements as alternatives that may better address underlying gendered organizational structures.
limitations: The study focused on corporately sponsored events in highly competitive, male-dominated industries in Germany and the UK, limiting generalizability to other industries, female-dominated fields, and non-Western cultural contexts. The authors' egalitarian cultural backgrounds may have caused them to miss subtle forms of benevolent sexism. Intersectional dynamics (race, disability, LGBT+ status, socioeconomic background) did not feature prominently and were not deeply examined.
future_research: Future work should examine women's networking in female-dominated fields, internal organizational networks tracked longitudinally, and events without corporate sponsorship to capture naturally occurring homophilic ties. Longitudinal studies could explore generational and cultural shifts, while cross-cultural comparative work can surface country-specific dynamics. Research on intersectionality and networking among other disadvantaged groups (by race, disability, or LGBT+ status), as well as mixed-gender networking events, would deepen understanding of context-specific homophily dynamics.
abstract: Although networking is critical for career advancement, prior research has primarily examined women’s networking in comparison to men’s, often concluding that it plays a less instrumental role due to gendered organizational structures. Women-only networking events are organized to enable women to leverage gender homophily for mutual support and career advancement. However, the mechanisms of gender homophily in women’s networking are not straightforward. Drawing on observations of women-only networking events, interviews, and secondary data, we identify three meanings women attach to homophilic networking (status-driven, instrumental, and expressive) and show how these meanings shape networking behaviors. In networking contexts characterized by “enforced homophily,” where support is offered under the guise of benevolence, women are more aware of these differences in meanings, which prevent them from leveraging the potential benefits of networking with other women. In contexts characterized by “agentic homophily,” the relevance of these differences is less salient, empowering women to challenge, rather than reproduce, gendered organizational structures.
apa_citation: Hein, P., Diehl, M.-R., & Kreutzer, K. (2025). Magic will happen if you gather us in one room: Gender homophily and women-only networking. *Journal of Management*. https://doi.org/10.1177/01492063251390850

title: How Deadly Is Financial Leverage? Evidence from Care Homes During the COVID-19 Crisis
methods: Cross-sectional and panel OLS regressions with care home and group fixed effects; propensity score matching; nonlinear (piecewise) specifications; extensive robustness/sensitivity analysis across alternative leverage constructions, samples, and wave definitions
industry: Care homes (nursing homes for older people)
time_period: COVID-19 pandemic, 2020 (first and second waves); financial accounts through fiscal year ending on or before 31 March 2020
units: Care homes and the corporate groups that operate them
n_sample: Working sample of 3,356 care homes operated by 1,045 providers with consolidated audited P&L accounts (broader sample: 7,585 care homes, 4,223 providers; universe: 8,760 care homes, 5,136 providers)
authors: Morris, P.; Phalippou, L.; Wu, B.
theories: agency theory (Jensen and Meckling 1976); financial distress / real effects of leverage; internal capital markets
keywords: COVID-19; IFRS 16; care homes; disclosure; healthcare; lease; leverage; real effects
research_question: How does financial leverage — once measured accurately by consolidating corporate group structures and capitalizing operating leases — affect resident death rates in English care homes during the COVID-19 pandemic, and does this relationship explain the previously documented higher mortality in private equity–controlled care homes?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on Jensen and Meckling (1976) and the broader real-effects-of-leverage literature, the authors argue that highly leveraged operators facing an unanticipated negative shock must cut costs aggressively to preserve debt-service capacity, and in a labor- and care-intensive industry these cost cuts (on PPE, staffing, agency workers) translate directly into higher resident mortality. Because financial flexibility depends on the consolidated group's balance sheet — including off-balance-sheet operating lease obligations — leverage measured at the standalone provider level or without lease capitalization badly mismeasures the true pressure on operators. Once leverage is reconstructed at the corporate-group level with operating leases capitalized (multiplied by 13x, based on REIT comparables), highly leveraged care homes show a first-wave death rate roughly double that of unleveraged care homes, the effect disappears in the second wave (when government support was in place), and the previously observed "PE effect" on mortality is fully absorbed by PE firms' higher leverage.
theoretical_contribution: The paper makes two contributions. First, it shows that accurate measurement of financial leverage — requiring consolidation at the corporate group level and capitalization of operating leases — is essential for detecting the real, non-financial externalities of debt, and that conventional provider- or brand-level, non-lease-adjusted leverage measures systematically understate financial pressure. Second, it demonstrates that the documented association between private equity ownership and worse stakeholder outcomes (care home mortality) operates primarily through the leverage channel rather than through ownership type per se, reframing the PE-and-healthcare debate as fundamentally a leverage-and-healthcare debate.
practical_implication: For policy makers and regulators (e.g., the UK Care Quality Commission), the paper argues that quality-of-care regulation should be complemented by systematic monitoring of providers' consolidated, lease-adjusted financial leverage, because high leverage generates negative stakeholder externalities during crises. For prospective residents and families trying to assess a care home's financial stability, currently available public data (Companies House, regulator filings) are insufficient without substantial accounting expertise to reconstruct group-level leverage. Accounting standard setters and disclosure regimes should take seriously the distortive effect of excluding operating leases for smaller or unquoted entities.
limitations: Propensity-score matching is difficult in this setting because high- and low-leverage care homes differ systematically on observables, so matches are loose. The lease-capitalization multiple (13x) is based on two UK care-home REITs and may not generalize. The analysis is confined to English care homes over a single pandemic episode, which, while offering strong identification, limits external generalizability. The leverage measure, although carefully constructed, still relies on self-reported financial accounts of varying quality (especially for smaller private entities).
future_research: The authors' framing implies several directions: replicating lease-adjusted, group-level leverage analyses in other jurisdictions and sectors (especially the United States, where data on private companies and operating leases are harder to obtain) to test whether the leverage-mortality channel generalizes beyond English care homes. The broader trend toward privatization of care services across countries invites cross-national comparative work on how ownership structure and financial leverage jointly shape outcomes in essential services. Further research could also examine whether the leverage channel observed here extends to other non-financial externalities of debt (e.g., quality of care in non-crisis periods, worker safety) and whether alternative accounting treatments of operating leases materially change prior empirical findings on leverage and real outcomes.
abstract: private equity firms no longer display higher COVID-19 death rates once their significantly higher leverage is controlled for.
apa_citation: Morris, P., Phalippou, L., & Wu, B. (2025). How deadly is financial leverage? Evidence from care homes during the COVID-19 crisis. *Management Science*, 72(3), 1769-1795. https://doi.org/10.1287/mnsc.2023.02923

title: Guest Editors’ Introduction: Redefining Organizational Ethics Through the Lens of Life-and-Death
methods: Editorial essay introducing a special issue; conceptual and normative argumentation, synthesis of philosophical, organizational, and posthumanist literatures, and framing of the six articles selected for the special issue
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
authors: Pérezts, M.; Fotaki, M.; Shymko, Y.; Islam, G.
theories: ethics of care; existential phenomenology; necropolitics; politics of inclusivity; posthumanism / more-than-human ethics
keywords: death; ethics of care and politics of inclusivity; life; organization and business ethics
research_question: Not reported in paper
mechanism_summary: The guest editors advance a normative framing rather than a causal model. They argue that organizing is never ethically neutral: routines, metrics, and rhetorics of efficiency distribute livability and non-livability across human and other-than-human actors, shaping who is enabled to flourish and who is "left to die" or consigned to "slow death." They propose that an organizational ethics of life-and-death must hold two poles in tension — the existential givenness of mortality and the political contingency of how lives are organized — and must be grounded in care, relationality, and collective solidarity rather than in shareholder or narrowly stakeholder logics. The essay uses this frame to read the six articles of the special issue as illustrations of how organizational practices, discourses, memes, bodies, and multispecies entanglements materially produce vital or deadly outcomes.
theoretical_contribution: The introduction reframes organizational and business ethics around "life-and-death" as a compound existential, collective, and posthuman category, extending Islam and Greenwood's (2021) call for a substantive (rather than narrow misconduct-focused) business ethics. Drawing on ethics of care, feminist relational thought, necropolitics, and more-than-human theorizing, the authors argue for an ethics that bridges micro-existential experience and macro-political organization, centers vulnerability and interdependence, and extends moral consideration beyond shareholders and even stakeholders to include nonhuman and not-yet-present others. They position this as a programmatic redefinition of the boundaries of business ethics in an era of intersecting ecological, geopolitical, and humanitarian crises.
practical_implication: The editors urge business ethics scholars, organizations, and managers to treat life-and-death stakes as core rather than peripheral to organizational decisions, resisting the sanitizing vocabularies of efficiency, "wellness," and neutral measurement that obscure who lives and who dies as a result of organizing. They call for organizing practices centered on care, relationality, collective solidarity, and multispecies accountability, and for visibilizing (rather than tabooing) death as part of ethical organizational life. Academia itself is implicated and should reflect on how its own infrastructures participate in life-and-death processes.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The editors sketch a research agenda organized around three lines of inquiry: principles of care and concern, collective solidarity, and accountability and relationality, including posthuman and multispecies extensions. They invite future work that broadens the geographies, voices, and scopes of the conversation, interrogates how measurement and datafication reconfigure life-and-death stakes, examines the role of academia in (re)producing livability, and develops organizational ethics capable of affirming life in the face of genocide, ecological collapse, and normalized "business as usual" forms of slow death.
abstract: We share the world we live and die in with others, in ways that are organized and disorganized. The authors of this special issue address life-and-death as a compound term, foregrounding the vital and deadly outcomes of (dis)organization and their (business) ethics implications as they play out in the context of growing inequalities and ongoing health, geopolitical, environmental, refugee crises and egregious war crimes. Organizations and organizing can shape such contexts by engaging in the ethics of care and politics of inclusivity, redefining “essential” or “front line” work, managing relationships between bodily health and work, or ethically relating to nonhuman forms of life. Considering the roles of organizations in terms of life-and-death can help scholars redefine organizations and/in/for/with the world by stressing the ethical dimensions of organizing for life which involves human and other-thanhuman relatedness and the obligation of care for all forms of life.
apa_citation: Pérezts, M., Fotaki, M., Shymko, Y., & Islam, G. (2025). Guest editors’ introduction: Redefining organizational ethics through the lens of life-and-death. *Business Ethics Quarterly*, 35(4), 502-524. https://doi.org/10.1017/beq.2025.10098

title: BOP’s dilemma: is jugaad an innovation or an invisible burden?
methods: Qualitative phenomenological study; 29 semi-structured in-depth interviews with BOP consumers; thematic analysis (open and axial coding) supported by NVIVO 14, yielding 115 open codes, 26 categories and nine themes
industry: Informal-sector occupations at the Bottom of the Pyramid (street vendors, laborers, barbers, shopkeepers, cleaners, cycle mechanics, homemakers, etc.)
country: India (Belpahar, Odisha and Kharagpur, West Bengal)
units: BOP consumers practicing jugaad
authors: Sutar, J.; Mukherjee, S.
theories: bounded rationality; expectation-confirmation model
keywords: Bottom of the Pyramid; Bounded rationality; Consequences; Continuance intention; Environmental risk; Expectation confirmation model; Health and safety risk; Jugaad; Social unacceptance
research_question: RQ1: What are the consequences of practicing jugaad at the BOP? RQ2: What motivates jugaad practitioners to continue with its usage despite adverse consequences?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on the expectation-confirmation model, the authors argue that BOP consumers form expectations about a jugaad solution at adoption and then evaluate post-use performance, with confirmation of expectations shaping satisfaction, perceived usefulness and continuance intention. However, they show that in BOP jugaad contexts this chain is disrupted in two ways: satisfied users may nevertheless abandon jugaad when health, safety or social-unacceptability risks materialize and override functional benefits; and dissatisfied users may persist because bounded rationality, financial scarcity and the absence of safer alternatives force them to satisfice rather than optimize, cognitively recalibrating expectations downward so that compromised functionality is accepted as "good enough." Jugaad is therefore reinterpreted as a necessity-driven, context-specific practice in which continuance reflects structural deprivation and social negotiation as much as positive evaluation.
theoretical_contribution: The study extends the expectation-confirmation model (ECM) to resource-constrained BOP settings by demonstrating a satisfaction–CI mismatch: satisfaction is a necessary but insufficient condition for continuance because health, safety and social-unacceptability consequences moderate the relationship, and dissatisfaction does not trigger discontinuance when viable alternatives are absent. It also extends bounded rationality theory into informal, survivalist BOP economies, showing that persistence with hazardous jugaad reflects satisficing under structural constraints rather than attitudinal endorsement. Finally, it unveils the social-acceptability and stigma dimensions of jugaad at the BOP, reframing it as a socially negotiated practice marked by aspiration and image management rather than a purely functional adaptation.
practical_implication: Businesses serving BOP markets should design affordable products that address both functional and aspirational needs, and can leverage the resourcefulness of jugaad for circular-economy branding and sustainability-driven segments. Policymakers, labor unions and social-initiative groups can reduce risks by running awareness campaigns, conducting safety audits in informal workplaces, training BOP consumers in safer alternatives, and institutionalizing affordable substitutes such as solar energy, low-cost water filtration and safer transport in place of hazardous electrical, water and vehicular jugaad. Municipal waste departments and urban planners can integrate jugaad practitioners into formal recycling and waste-management systems, while design-led capacity-building programs can elevate the cultural value of repurposed products and mitigate the stigma of jugaad as "the poor's way."
limitations: The study did not develop and validate measurement scales for the proposed constructs (environmental impact, social acceptability, maintenance costs), leaving the propositions untested in a structural model. The two regional sites (Odisha and West Bengal) turned out to be culturally similar, so intended cross-cultural comparisons were not achievable, and the sample is male-skewed because women were more reluctant to participate, limiting gendered insight. The cross-sectional design also cannot capture how jugaad practices and continuance intentions evolve as technologies, incomes and infrastructure change over time.
future_research: Future work should develop measurement scales and test the propositions as hypotheses in a structural model, and replicate the study in culturally distinct BOP regions to examine cross-cultural differences in jugaad. Researchers should pursue greater gender balance and examine gender as a moderator of jugaad practices, motivations and challenges in BOP communities. Longitudinal and comparative (urban vs. rural) designs are needed to track how jugaad and its social acceptability evolve as access to technology, education, infrastructure and awareness changes.
abstract: Purpose – This study aims to explore the consequences of jugaad at the Bottom of the Pyramid (BOP) and to examine the motivations behind continuance intention (CI) following its adoption. Design/methodology/approach – This qualitative phenomenological study used 29 in-depth interviews with BOP consumers. Thematic analysis yielded 26 categories and nine themes. The expectation-confirmation model (ECM) and bounded rationality (BR) serve as the theoretical lens through which the authors understand the rationale behind the CI of jugaad. Findings – Despite its utility, jugaad carries safety risks, frequent failures, and social stigma, putting BOP consumers in a dilemma of whether to persist. The study reveals a paradox where certain jugaad repurpose waste, while certain other jugaads pollute the environment. The study found that social unacceptance often outweighs functional benefits, showing that jugaad’s CI is shaped by nuanced, context-specific trade-offs. Originality/value – This study extends ECM by showing that BOP consumers may continue with jugaad despite dissatisfaction, driven by BR and limited alternatives. Satisfaction alone does not ensure CI. Safety concerns, social stigma, and aspirations shape CI. The findings offer key insights for policymakers and businesses engaging with BOP markets.
apa_citation: Sutar, J., & Mukherjee, S. (2025). BOP’s dilemma: Is jugaad an innovation or an invisible burden? *Journal of Consumer Marketing*. https://doi.org/10.1108/JCM-07-2025-8049

title: Beyond Dangling Carrots: The Effect of Policy Maker Motives on Their Response to Corporate Political Activity
methods: Conceptual theory-development paper drawing on social influence theory to build a policy-maker-centered framework of CPA; develops propositions about three motive types (instrumental, relational, moral), three CPA types, policy-maker type (politician vs. bureaucrat), and lobbyist intermediation.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
authors: Tabasi-Nejad, P.; Deutsch, Y.
theories: corporate political activity (political marketplace view); public service motivation; social influence theory
keywords: bureaucrats; corporate political activity; instrumental motives; lobbyists; moral motives; policy maker motives; politicians; relational motives; social influence theory
research_question: Why would a policy maker be influenced (or not) by a firm's CPA efforts? The authors develop a more comprehensive framework of CPA that centers the motives of the target policy maker rather than treating the policy maker as a solely instrumentally driven actor in an exchange.
mechanism_summary: Drawing on social influence theory, the paper argues that policy makers are driven not only by instrumental (self-interest) motives but also by relational motives (connection, belonging) and moral motives (values, identity). Firms can therefore engage in three corresponding CPA types, and effectiveness depends on the match between CPA type and the policy maker's dominant motive. Mismatches can backfire, as when instrumental inducements trigger moral identity threat in a morally motivated policy maker. Bureaucrats and politicians differ systematically in their motivational profiles and in how they process monitoring, relational salience, and moral outrage, so the same CPA tactic is not equally effective across policy-maker types. Third-party lobbyists enhance CPA by amplifying monitoring capacity, relational ties, and moral credibility, going beyond their currently theorized roles of resource aggregation and access provision.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends CPA theory beyond its exclusive focus on instrumental motives by integrating relational and moral motives, thereby identifying new noninstrumental types of CPA and explaining when CPA may succeed, fail, or even produce negative influence. It introduces policy-maker type (politician vs. bureaucrat) as a core theoretical component of CPA, arguing that bureaucrats constitute a motivationally distinct class of policy maker who have been largely neglected. It also expands the conceptualization of lobbyists from resource aggregators or access providers to key intermediaries who enhance influence determinants, and in doing so extends social influence theory to account for intermediaries and for influencee type.
practical_implication: For firms designing CPA strategies, the framework suggests that "dangling carrots" is often not the most effective tactic and can be counterproductive when policy makers are driven by relational or moral motives. Firms should diagnose the dominant motive profile of the target policy maker and match it with an appropriate CPA type, taking special care when targeting bureaucrats, who tend to differ from politicians in their motivational orientations. Employing third-party lobbyists is valuable not only for access but also for monitoring, relationship building, and enhancing moral credibility.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The authors invite research on CPA that simultaneously combines multiple influence types (e.g., relational and moral) and on which combinations are more likely to be used and succeed. They encourage empirical work on the relationship between corporate social responsibility and CPA when both speak to moral motives, and on how noninstrumental motives shape outcomes in settings beyond politics (e.g., incentive use in knowledge-sharing communities). They also call for research on how influencee type (e.g., bureaucrat-like, technically oriented stakeholders) shapes the effectiveness of relational influence, and on how intermediaries beyond lobbyists (e.g., lawyers serving activist shareholders) enhance influence in other contexts.
abstract: Management scholars have predominantly theorized corporate political activity (CPA) as an exchange wherein a firm offers resources to a policy maker in return for favorable policy. While the CPA literature has extensively explored what drives firms to engage in CPA, it remains largely silent on the question of why a policy maker would accede to a firm’s attempt at influence. Drawing on social influence theory, we develop a framework that extends current CPA theory and centers a policy maker’s perspective when faced with CPA. We introduce a policy maker’s noninstrumental motives and identify novel types of CPA that appeal to these motives. We also consider the role of the bureaucrat—a heretofore largely ignored but important type of policy maker whose motives differ from those of politicians in key respects that shape whether and how a given policy maker is influenced. Lastly, we examine how third-party lobbyists affect the CPA process, identifying key pathways through which they are likely to enhance a firm’s CPA. By theorizing around the motives of the policy maker faced with CPA, we present a novel policy maker-centered framework that helps us better understand this socially and economically important strategy.
apa_citation: Tabasi-Nejad, P., & Deutsch, Y. (2025). Beyond dangling carrots: The effect of policy maker motives on their response to corporate political activity. *Academy of Management Review*. https://doi.org/10.5465/amr.2023.0033

title: Getting Away with Murder: Legitimizing Unethical Organization Practices via Memes
methods: Inductive qualitative case study of Internet memes legitimizing three lethal Brazilian police operations (Jacarezinho, Vila Cruzeiro, Complexo do Alemão, 2021-2022); collection of 495 Instagram memes (feeds and stories) from police, politicians, and right-wing influencer profiles, and selection of seven most-engaged memes; discourse analysis following Shifman's (2013) and Gal et al.'s (2016) three-dimensional meme framework (form, content, stance), combined with intertextual tracing of cultural artefacts and traditional media references
industry: State policing / public security; social media (Instagram)
time_period: 2021-2022 (one-week collection windows following each of three police operations)
units: Internet memes circulated on Instagram in the aftermath of police massacres in Rio de Janeiro favelas
n_sample: 495 memes collected; 7 memes retained for in-depth discourse analysis (3 from Jacarezinho, 2 from Vila Cruzeiro, 2 from Complexo do Alemão)
authors: Vianna, F.; Alcadipani, R.; Barros, M.; Matarazzo, G.
theories: discursive legitimation; extreme case research; necrocapitalism; necropolitics; state of exception
keywords: ethics of life and death; favelas; legitimation; memes; police
research_question: How do social media memes legitimize organizational actions related to the ethics of life and death? The authors investigate this in the context of rising authoritarianism through the extreme case of Brazilian police massacres in Rio de Janeiro favelas.
mechanism_summary: Drawing on necropolitics and necrocapitalism, the paper argues that state organizations and their supporters use memes as a new legitimation technology that departs from the traditional discourses of normalization and invisibilization. Instead of hiding or routinizing killing, memes render deaths hyper-visible and celebrate them: by ridiculing human-rights critics, framing favela residents as deserving of death, and sanctifying police as rightful executioners, the memetic discourse constructs favelas as "states of exception" where lethal violence is morally warranted. The humorous, rapid, and ephemeral nature of memes short-circuits rational ethical debate and replaces it with superficial, affective engagement, thereby supporting racialized necropolitical regimes within a broader authoritarian drive enabled by social media platforms.
theoretical_contribution: The paper makes three contributions to the literature on the ethics of the organization of death. First, it identifies a third legitimation discourse alongside normalization and invisibilization — one that makes death visible and celebrates it, particularly when directed at marginalized groups. Second, it theorizes memes as a distinctive legitimation technology whose speed, humor, and ephemerality void ethical debate and replace it with vacuous engagement, linking social media affordances to the reproduction of authoritarian and necropolitical discourses. Third, it foregrounds the state's law-enforcement apparatus as an expression of racialized colonial inequalities, connecting business ethics to the critical study of state violence and the complicity of Big Tech platforms that allow such content to circulate.
practical_implication: The authors argue that ethical corporations, particularly Big Tech platforms, must account for the memetic content they allow to circulate, since permitting such material makes them complicit in the regulation of death and the terror inflicted on vulnerable populations. They also suggest that social media can be mobilized in the opposite direction — to combat inequalities in the organization of life and death, as seen in the George Floyd case — and call on business ethics practitioners, regulators, and civil society to contest memetic legitimations of state violence rather than treat them as harmless humor.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The authors call for further research on the organizational ethics of life and death in the post-truth era, particularly on the role of social media platforms and Big Tech complicity in enabling legitimations of killing. They invite studies that examine how memes and other pictorial technologies operate in other authoritarian and necropolitical contexts, and how counter-memes and alternative discourses might contest the celebration of death directed at marginalized and racialized populations.
abstract: Research on the ethics of life and death in organizations has overlooked the growing role of social media in shaping the ethical implications of organizational regulations on death. More specifically, while recent research suggests that memes are increasingly used to influence the reputation and perception of organizational actions, research on their roles in legitimating the organization of death is scant. This paper analyzes Internet memes legitimizing police slaughter in Brazilian favelas. Three primary discourses were identified: denying life worthiness, establishing actors deserving death, and upholding the police organization as executioners of death. This paper contributes to research on the ethics of organizational death in three different ways in a context of political polarization and growing authoritarism. First, it discusses how social media creates a legitimate discourse that makes death visible and gloats over its victims. Second, this discourse is enabled by social media memes, which divert the ethical debate on life and death, replacing it with superficial and transient engagement. Third, we discuss how memes can reinforce the role of the state police enforcement apparatus as an expression of racialized inequalities.
apa_citation: Vianna, F., Alcadipani, R., Barros, M., & Matarazzo, G. (2025). Getting away with murder: Legitimizing unethical organization practices via memes. *Business Ethics Quarterly*, 35(4), 620-651. https://doi.org/10.1017/beq.2025.10094

title: Participative pricing, food affordability, and nutritional adequacy: evidence from India
methods: Cross-sectional analysis of NSSO 2022-23 Consumption Expenditure Survey microdata; log-level OLS regressions, generalized linear models with log link, and logistic regressions; simulated PWYW (50/75/100/150% of baseline) and SYOP (+/-20% band) counterfactual pricing scenarios with interactions between pricing scenarios and MPCE income quintiles; separate urban/rural models; standard errors clustered at PSU level
industry: Household food consumption (all food categories: wholegrains, tubers, vegetables, fruits, dairy, protein, added fats, sugars, spices, processed)
units: Households (urban and rural) from NSSO HCES
n_sample: 261,746 households (155,014 rural and 106,732 urban); analytic sample 256,016
authors: Zeeshan, Z.; Khan, M. T.
theories: Engel's law; participative pricing (pay-what-you-want, set-your-own-price); price elasticity and consumer discretion
keywords: Affordability; Consumer behavior; Food expenditure; Income elasticity; India; NSSO; Nutritional adequacy; Participative pricing
research_question: How do participative pricing mechanisms (PWYW and SYOP) influence household food expenditure patterns across income groups; can they improve affordability for diverse and nutritious food categories; and how do participative pricing responses differ across urban and rural households in India?
mechanism_summary: Participative pricing shifts part of the pricing decision from the seller to the buyer, introducing consumer discretion over prices. Under PWYW, simulated fixed-fraction discounts interact with existing affordability constraints: lower prices do not automatically raise quantities because households reallocate their budgets across food categories, with urban and higher-income households being more elastic than rural or low-income ones. Under SYOP, consumer-set prices within a narrow acceptance band allow higher-income consumers to channel spending toward nutrient-dense foods, but binding affordability constraints prevent lower-income households from doing the same. The heterogeneity across income and sector reflects differences in budget slack, market access and price elasticity rooted in Engel's-law reasoning about food shares and household welfare.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends participative pricing research beyond digital commerce and cultural industries to food affordability in a developing-country context, formalizing PWYW and SYOP as counterfactual pricing regimes over national survey microdata. It reframes the policy question from "how much to cut prices" toward "how to allocate pricing discretion and complementary access supports," showing that bounded consumer discretion (SYOP) outperforms uniform discounts (PWYW) for affordability when budgets are tight. In doing so, it integrates participative pricing with nutrition-transition and food-security literatures in low- and middle-income countries.
practical_implication: Policymakers and retailers should pair flexible pricing with complementary interventions: tiered "community pricing" bands anchored by a suggested price, simple eligibility (e.g. ration-card verification) for reduced bands and brief SYOP windows to learn willingness-to-pay. Urban, higher-income households respond to PWYW-style discounts, while rural and low-income households require structural supports (market access, subsidies, income transfers) to benefit. Programs should track affordability rates (<=30% MPCE), basket diversity and sector-specific take-up, iterating floors and bands accordingly.
limitations: Price variation is simulated through PWYW fractions and SYOP bands rather than observed in live markets, so external validity depends on how floors, reference prices and eligibility are implemented in practice. The analysis focuses on expenditure rather than directly mapping quantities to nutrients, which would require additional assumptions beyond the scope of the study. The design is cross-sectional, precluding observation of learning, persistence or seasonal adjustment in consumer behavior.
future_research: Future work should run live pilots or staggered rollouts to capture dynamic learning, persistence and seasonal effects under actual participative pricing. Short panels could identify how floors, reference prices and eligibility rules shape affordability and basket composition. Extensions could map expenditure changes more explicitly to nutritional intake and calorie adequacy, and test community pricing bands in field experiments across regions.
abstract: Purpose – This study investigates the impact of participatory pricing mechanisms, specifically pay-what-you-want (PWYW) and set-your-own-price (SYOP), on household food expenditure and affordability in India. This study aims to understand how these pricing models affect consumer behavior across various food categories and income groups in both urban and rural settings. Design/methodology/approach – Using data from the NSSO 2022–23 Consumption Expenditure Survey (CES), this study uses log-level ordinary least squares regressions, generalized linear models with a log link and logistic regressions. These models incorporate interactions between pricing scenarios (for PWYW, as well as SYOP quintiles) and income groups (measured by monthly per capita expenditure quintiles). A comprehensive set of control variables at the household level was included to ensure robust estimates. Findings – The results indicate that PWYW pricing leads to nonlinear changes in food expenditure. Urban households exhibit elastic responses, with higher-income groups reallocating their spending more significantly in response to discounts, whereas rural households display inelastic behavior under similar pricing conditions. Under the SYOP model, lower-income households face severe affordability constraints, whereas higher-income consumers demonstrate increased spending on nutrient-dense foods. These heterogeneous responses suggest that price flexibility alone may not uniformly improve food affordability and nutritional adequacy across different socio-economic contexts. Originality/value – This research extends the existing literature on pricing strategies by applying participative pricing models to food expenditure and nutritional adequacy in a developing country context. The study offers actionable insights for policymakers and industry stakeholders, underscoring the need for targeted interventions that integrate flexible pricing with complementary social and economic support measures.
apa_citation: Zeeshan, Z., & Khan, M. T. (2025). Participative pricing, food affordability, and nutritional adequacy: Evidence from India. *Journal of Consumer Marketing*. https://doi.org/10.1108/JCM-02-2025-7667
